Cybersecurity.explanation: STRIDE: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Info disclosure, DoS, Elevation of privilege.

Cybersecurity.question: What is the principle of least privilege?
options: B) Users and systems get minimum permissions needed for their function — reduces attack surface
Cybersecurity.correctAnswer: B) Users and systems get minimum permissions needed for their function — reduces attack surface
Cybersecurity.explanation: If microservice only reads data, don't give it write permissions. Limits damage if compromised.

AI_ML.question: What is supervised vs unsupervised learning?
options: B) Supervised: labeled training data with targets; Unsupervised: finds patterns in unlabeled data
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Supervised: labeled training data with targets; Unsupervised: finds patterns in unlabeled data
AI_ML.explanation: Supervised: classification, regression. Unsupervised: clustering, dimensionality reduction, anomaly detection.

AI_ML.question: What is gradient descent?
options: B) Optimization algorithm iteratively adjusting parameters in direction of steepest loss decrease
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Optimization algorithm iteratively adjusting parameters in direction of steepest loss decrease
AI_ML.explanation: ∂Loss/∂weights tells slope. Move in opposite direction. Learning rate controls step size.

AI_ML.question: What is backpropagation?
options: B) Algorithm computing gradients layer-by-layer using chain rule — enables training deep networks
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Algorithm computing gradients layer-by-layer using chain rule — enables training deep networks
AI_ML.explanation: Forward pass computes output. Backward pass computes gradients. Optimizer updates weights.

AI_ML.question: What is overfitting vs underfitting?
options: B) Overfitting: memorizes training data fails on new; Underfitting: too simple fails on both
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Overfitting: memorizes training data fails on new; Underfitting: too simple fails on both
AI_ML.explanation: Fix overfitting: more data, dropout, regularization, simpler model. Fix underfitting: more features, complex model.

AI_ML.question: What is the transformer architecture?
options: B) Attention-based architecture using self-attention mechanisms — foundation of all modern LLMs
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Attention-based architecture using self-attention mechanisms — foundation of all modern LLMs
AI_ML.explanation: Self-attention allows attending to all positions simultaneously. Multi-head attention captures different relationships.

AI_ML.question: What is attention mechanism in transformers?
options: B) Query-Key-Value mechanism: attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k)V — each token attends to all others
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Query-Key-Value mechanism: attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k)V — each token attends to all others
AI_ML.explanation: √d_k scaling prevents dot products from getting too large in high dimensions.

AI_ML.question: What is transfer learning?
options: B) Using pre-trained model on new task — fine-tune existing weights rather than train from scratch
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Using pre-trained model on new task — fine-tune existing weights rather than train from scratch
AI_ML.explanation: GPT is pre-trained on internet, fine-tuned for specific tasks. Reduces data and compute requirements dramatically.

AI_ML.question: What is regularization?
options: B) Techniques reducing overfitting: L1/L2 add penalty to loss; dropout randomly zeros neurons
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Techniques reducing overfitting: L1/L2 add penalty to loss; dropout randomly zeros neurons
AI_ML.explanation: L1 (Lasso): sparsity, feature selection. L2 (Ridge): shrink weights. Dropout: ensemble effect.

AI_ML.question: What is batch normalization?
options: B) Normalizes layer inputs per mini-batch — reduces internal covariate shift, speeds training
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Normalizes layer inputs per mini-batch — reduces internal covariate shift, speeds training
AI_ML.explanation: Apply after linear layer, before activation. Allows higher learning rates, acts as regularizer.

AI_ML.question: What is the vanishing gradient problem?
options: B) Gradients become exponentially small in early layers — training stalls in deep networks
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Gradients become exponentially small in early layers — training stalls in deep networks
AI_ML.explanation: Solutions: ReLU activations, residual connections (ResNet), batch normalization, LSTM for sequences.

AI_ML.question: What is RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)?
options: B) Enhancing LLM with retrieved relevant documents — grounds responses in factual external knowledge
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Enhancing LLM with retrieved relevant documents — grounds responses in factual external knowledge
AI_ML.explanation: Fetch relevant docs via vector search → inject into context → LLM generates grounded answer. Reduces hallucination.

AI_ML.question: What is fine-tuning vs prompting?
options: B) Fine-tuning: update model weights on task-specific data; Prompting: guide behavior via input only
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Fine-tuning: update model weights on task-specific data; Prompting: guide behavior via input only
AI_ML.explanation: Try prompting first — cheaper and faster. Fine-tune when prompting can't achieve needed quality/style.

AI_ML.question: What is a vector embedding?
options: B) Dense numerical representation of data in high-dimensional space — semantic similarity = vector proximity
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Dense numerical representation of data in high-dimensional space — semantic similarity = vector proximity
AI_ML.explanation: word2vec: king - man + woman ≈ queen. Text embeddings power semantic search and RAG.

AI_ML.question: What is a vector database?
options: B) Specialized database for similarity search on embeddings — Pinecone, Chroma, Weaviate, pgvector
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Specialized database for similarity search on embeddings — Pinecone, Chroma, Weaviate, pgvector
AI_ML.explanation: Stores vectors and finds nearest neighbors efficiently using HNSW or IVF indexes.

AI_ML.question: What is temperature in LLMs?
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Controls randomness of output — 0=deterministic, 1=balanced, >1=creative/random
AI_ML.explanation: Low temperature for factual tasks (0.1-0.3). High for creative writing (0.7-1.0). Applied to softmax logits.

AI_ML.question: What is prompt injection?
options: B) Attacker manipulates LLM behavior through carefully crafted inputs overriding system instructions
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Attacker manipulates LLM behavior through carefully crafted inputs overriding system instructions
AI_ML.explanation: 'Ignore previous instructions...' — LLMs can't cryptographically separate instructions from data yet.

AI_ML.question: What is RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)?
options: B) Training LLMs to align with human preferences using human evaluators and PPO algorithm
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Training LLMs to align with human preferences using human evaluators and PPO algorithm
AI_ML.explanation: Used by OpenAI for ChatGPT. Humans rank responses → reward model trained → LLM optimized via PPO.

AI_ML.question: What is the context window in LLMs?
options: B) Maximum tokens model can process at once — determines how much text model 'remembers'
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Maximum tokens model can process at once — determines how much text model 'remembers'
AI_ML.explanation: GPT-4: 128k tokens. Claude: 200k. Longer context = more expensive. RAG extends effective context.

AI_ML.question: What is LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)?
options: B) Parameter-efficient fine-tuning — adds small trainable matrices, keeps base model frozen
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Parameter-efficient fine-tuning — adds small trainable matrices, keeps base model frozen
AI_ML.explanation: LoRA reduces trainable params by 10,000x — fine-tune GPT on consumer GPU. QLoRA adds 4-bit quantization.

AI_ML.question: What is chain-of-thought prompting?
options: B) Prompting LLM to show reasoning steps before answer — dramatically improves complex problem solving
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Prompting LLM to show reasoning steps before answer — dramatically improves complex problem solving
AI_ML.explanation: 'Let's think step by step...' or few-shot examples with reasoning. Enables models to solve math/logic.

AgenticAI.question: What is an AI agent?
options: B) Autonomous system perceiving environment, making decisions, taking actions to achieve goals
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Autonomous system perceiving environment, making decisions, taking actions to achieve goals
AgenticAI.explanation: Agents combine LLMs with tools, memory, and planning to autonomously complete multi-step tasks.

AgenticAI.question: What is ReAct (Reasoning + Acting)?
options: B) Agent pattern interleaving reasoning traces and actions — LLM thinks then acts iteratively
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Agent pattern interleaving reasoning traces and actions — LLM thinks then acts iteratively
AgenticAI.explanation: Thought: I need to search X. Action: search(X). Observation: result. Thought: now I know... — iterative loop.

AgenticAI.question: What is tool calling / function calling in agents?
options: B) LLM outputs structured JSON describing function to call — framework executes and returns result
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) LLM outputs structured JSON describing function to call — framework executes and returns result
AgenticAI.explanation: LLM decides: {name: 'search', args: {query: 'weather NYC'}}. Framework calls search(), returns to LLM.

AgenticAI.question: What is agent memory architecture?
options: B) Short-term (context window), long-term (vector DB), episodic (past interactions), semantic (facts)
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Short-term (context window), long-term (vector DB), episodic (past interactions), semantic (facts)
AgenticAI.explanation: Short-term limited by context. Long-term via RAG retrieval. Episodic stores past conversations.

AgenticAI.question: What is the ReAct vs Plan-and-Execute pattern?
options: B) ReAct: interleaved think-act; Plan-and-Execute: plan entire sequence first, then execute steps
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) ReAct: interleaved think-act; Plan-and-Execute: plan entire sequence first, then execute steps
AgenticAI.explanation: Plan-and-Execute better for long tasks with clear steps. ReAct better for exploratory/adaptive tasks.

AgenticAI.question: What is LangChain?
options: B) Framework for building LLM applications — chains, agents, tools, memory, RAG abstractions
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Framework for building LLM applications — chains, agents, tools, memory, RAG abstractions
AgenticAI.explanation: LangChain provides standardized interfaces for LLMs, vector stores, tools enabling complex pipelines.

AgenticAI.question: What is the difference between chain and agent in LangChain?
options: B) Chain: fixed predetermined steps; Agent: LLM dynamically decides which tools to use
D) Chain uses LLM; Agent doesn't
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Chain: fixed predetermined steps; Agent: LLM dynamically decides which tools to use
AgenticAI.explanation: Use chains for predictable workflows. Use agents when decision logic is complex or dynamic.

AgenticAI.question: What is a multi-agent system?
options: B) Multiple specialized agents collaborating — each has specific role, communicate to solve complex tasks
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Multiple specialized agents collaborating — each has specific role, communicate to solve complex tasks
AgenticAI.explanation: AutoGen, CrewAI patterns: orchestrator delegates to specialist agents (researcher, coder, reviewer).

AgenticAI.question: What is agent hallucination and mitigation?
options: B) Agent confidently takes wrong action — mitigate with tool verification, human-in-loop, self-reflection
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Agent confidently takes wrong action — mitigate with tool verification, human-in-loop, self-reflection
AgenticAI.explanation: Grounding via tools, retrieval, structured outputs reduce hallucination. Human approval for irreversible actions.

AgenticAI.question: What is a tool in agent context?
options: B) Function agent can call to interact with world — web search, code execution, DB queries, APIs
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Function agent can call to interact with world — web search, code execution, DB queries, APIs
AgenticAI.explanation: Tools are agent's hands. Careful tool design with clear descriptions improves LLM tool selection accuracy.

AgenticAI.question: What is structured output in agents?
options: B) Constraining LLM output to specific schema — ensures parseable reliable tool calls and decisions
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Constraining LLM output to specific schema — ensures parseable reliable tool calls and decisions
AgenticAI.explanation: Pydantic models, JSON schema, instructor library — force LLM to output valid structured data.

AgenticAI.question: What is an agent loop / agentic loop?
options: B) Observe → Think → Act cycle repeating until goal achieved or stop condition met
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Observe → Think → Act cycle repeating until goal achieved or stop condition met
AgenticAI.explanation: Each iteration: get observations, reason about state, pick action, execute, observe result, repeat.

AgenticAI.question: What is agent evaluation (evals)?
options: B) Systematic measurement of agent performance — task completion, efficiency, accuracy, safety
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Systematic measurement of agent performance — task completion, efficiency, accuracy, safety
AgenticAI.explanation: Build eval harness before optimizing. Track: success rate, tool call efficiency, hallucination rate, cost.

AgenticAI.question: What is the AutoGen framework?
options: B) Microsoft framework for multi-agent conversations — agents converse with each other to solve tasks
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Microsoft framework for multi-agent conversations — agents converse with each other to solve tasks
AgenticAI.explanation: AssistantAgent + UserProxyAgent pattern. Agents can write and execute code collaboratively.

AgenticAI.question: What is the Model Context Protocol (MCP)?
options: B) Anthropic's standardized protocol for connecting LLMs to tools and data sources
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Anthropic's standardized protocol for connecting LLMs to tools and data sources
AgenticAI.explanation: MCP standardizes how AI assistants connect to tools — like USB-C for AI. Enables reusable tool servers.

AgenticAI.question: What is a system prompt in agent design?
options: B) Persistent instructions defining agent identity, capabilities, constraints, and behavioral guidelines
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Persistent instructions defining agent identity, capabilities, constraints, and behavioral guidelines
AgenticAI.explanation: Well-crafted system prompts are crucial for agent reliability. Define role, tools, output format, safety rules.

AgenticAI.question: What is OpenAI Assistants API?
options: B) Managed agent infrastructure — threads, messages, runs, tool execution handled by OpenAI
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Managed agent infrastructure — threads, messages, runs, tool execution handled by OpenAI
AgenticAI.explanation: Handles: persistent threads, file attachments, code interpreter, retrieval, function calling — batteries included.

AgenticAI.question: What is semantic kernel?
options: B) Microsoft SDK connecting LLMs with plugins and memories for AI application development
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Microsoft SDK connecting LLMs with plugins and memories for AI application development
AgenticAI.explanation: SK provides skills/plugins, memory connectors, planners for enterprise AI applications in C#, Python, Java.

AgenticAI.question: What is task decomposition in agents?
options: B) Breaking complex task into subtasks assignable to tools or sub-agents — enables complex automation
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Breaking complex task into subtasks assignable to tools or sub-agents — enables complex automation
AgenticAI.explanation: 'Write a research report' → search, read papers, synthesize, write sections, review — decomposed steps.

AgenticAI.question: What is self-reflection in AI agents?
options: B) Agent critiques its own outputs and plans, iterating to improve before final answer
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Agent critiques its own outputs and plans, iterating to improve before final answer
AgenticAI.explanation: Reflexion paper: agent reflects on failures, stores reflections in memory, avoids repeating mistakes.

HTML.question: What does HTML stand for?
HTML.correctAnswer: A) Hyper Text Markup Language
HTML.explanation: HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language — the standard language for creating web pages.

HTML.question: Which tag creates a hyperlink?
HTML.correctAnswer: B) <a>
HTML.explanation: The <a> (anchor) tag with href attribute creates hyperlinks.

HTML.question: What is the correct HTML for the largest heading?
HTML.explanation: <h1> is the largest heading tag, <h6> is the smallest.

HTML.question: Which HTML attribute specifies an alternate text for an image?
HTML.correctAnswer: C) alt
HTML.explanation: The alt attribute provides alternative text for screen readers and when image fails to load.

HTML.question: What does the DOCTYPE declaration do?
options: B) Tells browser which HTML version is being used
HTML.correctAnswer: B) Tells browser which HTML version is being used
HTML.explanation: <!DOCTYPE html> tells the browser to use HTML5 rendering mode.

HTML.question: Which tag is used for an unordered list?
HTML.correctAnswer: C) <ul>
HTML.explanation: <ul> creates unordered (bulleted) lists, <ol> creates ordered (numbered) lists.

HTML.question: What is semantic HTML?
options: B) Using HTML elements that convey meaning about content structure
HTML.correctAnswer: B) Using HTML elements that convey meaning about content structure
HTML.explanation: Semantic elements like <article>, <nav>, <section> describe their content, improving accessibility and SEO.

HTML.question: Which attribute makes an input field required?
HTML.correctAnswer: C) required
HTML.explanation: The required attribute prevents form submission if the field is empty.

HTML.question: What is the difference between <div> and <span>?
options: B) div is block-level; span is inline
C) span is block-level; div is inline
D) div is for text; span is for images
HTML.correctAnswer: B) div is block-level; span is inline
HTML.explanation: Block elements start on new line and take full width. Inline elements flow within text.

HTML.question: What does the <meta charset='UTF-8'> tag do?
options: B) Defines character encoding for the document
HTML.correctAnswer: B) Defines character encoding for the document
HTML.explanation: UTF-8 encoding supports virtually all characters and is the standard for web pages.

CSS.question: What is the CSS box model?
options: B) content + padding + border + margin — defines element's space and dimensions
CSS.correctAnswer: B) content + padding + border + margin — defines element's space and dimensions
CSS.explanation: Every element is a box. Understanding content/padding/border/margin is fundamental to layout.

CSS.question: What is the difference between box-sizing: content-box vs border-box?
options: B) content-box: width=content only; border-box: width includes padding+border
D) content-box is default in modern browsers
CSS.correctAnswer: B) content-box: width=content only; border-box: width includes padding+border
CSS.explanation: border-box makes sizing intuitive — a 200px box is always 200px regardless of padding/border.

CSS.question: What is CSS specificity order (highest to lowest)?
CSS.correctAnswer: B) inline > ID > class/pseudo-class > element
CSS.explanation: Inline styles override all. ID selectors > class selectors > element selectors.

CSS.question: What is the difference between Flexbox and Grid?
options: B) Flexbox is 1D (row or column); Grid is 2D (rows AND columns simultaneously)
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Flexbox is 1D (row or column); Grid is 2D (rows AND columns simultaneously)
CSS.explanation: Use Flexbox for single-axis layouts. Use Grid for complex 2D page layouts.

CSS.question: What does position: sticky do?
options: B) Scrolls normally until hitting threshold, then sticks in place
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Scrolls normally until hitting threshold, then sticks in place
CSS.explanation: Sticky is a hybrid of relative and fixed — sticks within its scroll container.

CSS.question: What is a CSS pseudo-element?
options: B) Keywords like ::before, ::after that style specific parts of element
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Keywords like ::before, ::after that style specific parts of element
CSS.explanation: ::before and ::after inject content before/after element. ::first-line styles first line of text.

CSS.question: What is z-index and what does it require?
options: B) Controls stacking order — only works on positioned elements (not static)
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Controls stacking order — only works on positioned elements (not static)
CSS.explanation: For z-index to work, element must have position: relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky.

CSS.question: What is a CSS custom property (variable)?
options: B) --name: value variables accessed with var(--name), scoped to selector
CSS.correctAnswer: B) --name: value variables accessed with var(--name), scoped to selector
CSS.explanation: CSS variables enable theming, are reactive to changes, and cascade through the DOM.

CSS.question: What is the difference between em and rem units?
options: B) em relative to parent's font-size; rem relative to root (html) font-size
C) rem is relative to parent
CSS.correctAnswer: B) em relative to parent's font-size; rem relative to root (html) font-size
CSS.explanation: rem is more predictable for font-sizes as it doesn't compound with nested elements.

CSS.question: What is CSS transform vs transition?
options: B) transform changes geometry (rotate/scale/translate); transition animates property change over time
CSS.correctAnswer: B) transform changes geometry (rotate/scale/translate); transition animates property change over time
CSS.explanation: Combine both: use transform for what to change, transition for how long the change takes.

JavaScript.question: What is the output of typeof null?
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) object
JavaScript.explanation: This is a famous JS bug. typeof null returns 'object' — has been kept for backward compatibility.

JavaScript.question: What is a closure?
options: B) Function retaining access to outer scope variables even after outer function returns
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Function retaining access to outer scope variables even after outer function returns
JavaScript.explanation: Closures enable data encapsulation, module patterns, and callbacks with preserved context.

JavaScript.question: What is the difference between let, const, and var?
options: B) var is function-scoped hoisted; let/const are block-scoped; const can't be reassigned
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) var is function-scoped hoisted; let/const are block-scoped; const can't be reassigned
JavaScript.explanation: Prefer const by default, let when reassignment needed, avoid var due to hoisting confusion.

JavaScript.question: What is event delegation?
options: B) Adding single listener to parent to handle events from all children via bubbling
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Adding single listener to parent to handle events from all children via bubbling
JavaScript.explanation: Event delegation improves performance for dynamic lists — one handler instead of hundreds.

JavaScript.question: What is the prototype chain?
options: B) Mechanism where objects inherit properties from their prototype object up the chain to null
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Mechanism where objects inherit properties from their prototype object up the chain to null
JavaScript.explanation: When accessing property, JS looks up prototype chain until found or reaches null.

JavaScript.question: What is the event loop?
options: B) Single-threaded mechanism: call stack + web APIs + callback queue + microtask queue
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Single-threaded mechanism: call stack + web APIs + callback queue + microtask queue
JavaScript.explanation: Event loop enables non-blocking I/O by offloading to Web APIs and processing callbacks.

JavaScript.question: Microtasks vs macrotasks — execution order?
options: B) After each task: ALL microtasks drain first (Promises), then one macrotask (setTimeout)
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) After each task: ALL microtasks drain first (Promises), then one macrotask (setTimeout)
JavaScript.explanation: Promise.then callbacks are microtasks — they run before setTimeout callbacks always.

JavaScript.question: What does async/await compile to?
options: B) Syntactic sugar over Promises — async returns Promise, await pauses execution until resolved
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Syntactic sugar over Promises — async returns Promise, await pauses execution until resolved
JavaScript.explanation: async/await makes async code readable. Under the hood, it still uses Promises.

JavaScript.question: What is the difference between == and ===?
options: B) == coerces types then compares; === compares value AND type without coercion
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) == coerces types then compares; === compares value AND type without coercion
JavaScript.explanation: Always use === to avoid subtle bugs from type coercion.

JavaScript.question: What is memoization?
options: B) Optimization caching function results — same inputs return cached output avoiding recomputation
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Optimization caching function results — same inputs return cached output avoiding recomputation
JavaScript.explanation: Useful for expensive pure functions called repeatedly with same arguments.

NodeJS.question: What is Node.js?
options: B) JavaScript runtime built on V8 enabling server-side execution with non-blocking I/O
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) JavaScript runtime built on V8 enabling server-side execution with non-blocking I/O
NodeJS.explanation: Node's event-driven, non-blocking model makes it efficient for I/O-heavy applications.

NodeJS.question: What is the difference between require() and import?
options: B) require() is CommonJS synchronous dynamic; import is ES modules static async tree-shakeable
D) require() is for JSON only
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) require() is CommonJS synchronous dynamic; import is ES modules static async tree-shakeable
NodeJS.explanation: Use .mjs or 'type':'module' in package.json for ES modules in Node.

NodeJS.question: What is the EventEmitter?
options: B) Core class for event-driven architecture — on() to subscribe, emit() to trigger events
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Core class for event-driven architecture — on() to subscribe, emit() to trigger events
NodeJS.explanation: Most Node.js core modules extend EventEmitter — streams, HTTP server, etc.

NodeJS.question: What is a Node.js stream?
options: B) Abstract interface for reading/writing data in chunks — memory efficient for large files
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Abstract interface for reading/writing data in chunks — memory efficient for large files
NodeJS.explanation: 4 types: Readable, Writable, Duplex, Transform. Pipe them for efficient data processing.

NodeJS.question: What is the cluster module?
options: B) Creates child processes sharing server port — utilizes multiple CPU cores
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Creates child processes sharing server port — utilizes multiple CPU cores
NodeJS.explanation: Since Node is single-threaded, cluster module spawns workers equal to CPU cores for load balancing.

NodeJS.question: What is libuv in Node.js?
options: B) C library providing async I/O, event loop, and thread pool to Node.js
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) C library providing async I/O, event loop, and thread pool to Node.js
NodeJS.explanation: libuv's thread pool handles file system ops and DNS that can't be done asynchronously by OS.

NodeJS.question: What is the difference between process.nextTick() and setImmediate()?
options: B) nextTick runs before I/O callbacks (same phase); setImmediate runs in check phase after I/O
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) nextTick runs before I/O callbacks (same phase); setImmediate runs in check phase after I/O
NodeJS.explanation: Use nextTick for critical callbacks. Use setImmediate to yield to I/O callbacks first.

NodeJS.question: What is middleware in Express?
options: B) Function(req, res, next) executing between request and route handler
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Function(req, res, next) executing between request and route handler
NodeJS.explanation: Middleware chain processes request: authentication, logging, parsing, validation before handler.

NodeJS.question: What is JWT and its structure?
options: B) JSON Web Token: header.payload.signature — stateless authentication token
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) JSON Web Token: header.payload.signature — stateless authentication token
NodeJS.explanation: JWT is signed (not encrypted by default). Never store sensitive data in payload — it's base64 decoded easily.

NodeJS.question: What is bcrypt and why use it for passwords?
options: B) Password hashing with salt — deliberately slow to prevent brute force attacks
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Password hashing with salt — deliberately slow to prevent brute force attacks
NodeJS.explanation: bcrypt's cost factor makes it exponentially slower per guess — 12 rounds is standard.

React.question: What is the Virtual DOM?
options: B) In-memory JS representation of real DOM — React diffs it to minimize actual DOM mutations
React.correctAnswer: B) In-memory JS representation of real DOM — React diffs it to minimize actual DOM mutations
React.explanation: Virtual DOM enables declarative programming — React handles efficient DOM updates automatically.

React.question: What triggers a React re-render?
options: B) State change, prop change, parent re-render, or context change
React.correctAnswer: B) State change, prop change, parent re-render, or context change
React.explanation: Understanding what causes re-renders is key to React performance optimization.

React.question: What is the difference between useState and useRef?
options: B) useState triggers re-render on update; useRef persists value without triggering re-render
React.correctAnswer: B) useState triggers re-render on update; useRef persists value without triggering re-render
React.explanation: Use useRef for DOM references, timers, previous values — anything that shouldn't cause re-render.

React.question: What does empty [] dependency array in useEffect mean?
options: B) Runs only once after initial mount — like componentDidMount
React.correctAnswer: B) Runs only once after initial mount — like componentDidMount
React.explanation: No deps = run once. Deps array = run when deps change. No array = run every render.

React.question: What is the stale closure problem?
options: B) Hook captures outdated value from previous render — fixed by adding to dependency array
React.correctAnswer: B) Hook captures outdated value from previous render — fixed by adding to dependency array
React.explanation: Classic bug: setInterval inside useEffect with empty deps reads stale state forever.

React.question: What is useMemo vs useCallback?
options: B) useMemo memoizes computed VALUE; useCallback memoizes FUNCTION reference
React.correctAnswer: B) useMemo memoizes computed VALUE; useCallback memoizes FUNCTION reference
React.explanation: Use useMemo for expensive calculations. Use useCallback to stabilize function identity passed as props.

React.question: What is React reconciliation?
options: B) Diffing algorithm comparing old vs new virtual DOM to determine minimal real DOM updates
React.correctAnswer: B) Diffing algorithm comparing old vs new virtual DOM to determine minimal real DOM updates
React.explanation: React's O(n) reconciliation algorithm uses element type and key prop to efficiently update DOM.

React.question: What is React.memo()?
options: B) HOC memoizing component — skips re-render if props haven't changed (shallow comparison)
React.correctAnswer: B) HOC memoizing component — skips re-render if props haven't changed (shallow comparison)
React.explanation: React.memo prevents re-renders when parent re-renders but props are unchanged.

React.question: What is useReducer vs useState?
options: B) useReducer better for complex state with multiple sub-values or next state depending on previous
React.correctAnswer: B) useReducer better for complex state with multiple sub-values or next state depending on previous
React.explanation: When state transitions are complex (like Redux), useReducer with dispatch/action is cleaner.

React.question: What is React.lazy() and Suspense?
options: B) Code splitting: lazy() dynamically imports component; Suspense shows fallback while loading
React.correctAnswer: B) Code splitting: lazy() dynamically imports component; Suspense shows fallback while loading
React.explanation: Reduces initial bundle size by loading components only when needed.

SQL.question: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?
options: B) WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY
C) HAVING filters before GROUP BY
SQL.correctAnswer: B) WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY
SQL.explanation: WHERE can't use aggregate functions. HAVING is specifically for filtering aggregated results.

SQL.question: What is a database index and trade-offs?
options: B) Data structure speeding up reads — trade-off: slower writes and inserts, more storage
SQL.correctAnswer: B) Data structure speeding up reads — trade-off: slower writes and inserts, more storage
SQL.explanation: Index every WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY column. Too many indexes slow INSERT/UPDATE significantly.

SQL.question: What is database normalization?
options: B) Organizing tables eliminating redundancy — 1NF atomic, 2NF no partial deps, 3NF no transitive deps
SQL.correctAnswer: B) Organizing tables eliminating redundancy — 1NF atomic, 2NF no partial deps, 3NF no transitive deps
SQL.explanation: Normalize to 3NF typically. Denormalize selectively for read performance when needed.

SQL.question: What is INNER JOIN vs LEFT JOIN?
options: B) INNER: only matching rows; LEFT: all left rows plus matching right (NULL if no match)
C) LEFT only returns left table
SQL.correctAnswer: B) INNER: only matching rows; LEFT: all left rows plus matching right (NULL if no match)
SQL.explanation: LEFT JOIN is most common — use to include records even when related record doesn't exist.

SQL.question: What is a CTE (Common Table Expression)?
options: B) Named temporary result with WITH clause — makes complex queries readable and reusable in query
SQL.correctAnswer: B) Named temporary result with WITH clause — makes complex queries readable and reusable in query
SQL.explanation: CTEs can reference themselves (recursive CTEs) for hierarchical data like org charts.

SQL.question: What is a window function?
options: B) Calculation across related rows without collapsing them — ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, SUM OVER
SQL.correctAnswer: B) Calculation across related rows without collapsing them — ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, SUM OVER
SQL.explanation: OVER(PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary) calculates within groups while keeping all rows.

SQL.question: What is a transaction and ACID?
options: B) Atomic: all-or-nothing; Consistent: rules maintained; Isolated: concurrent safe; Durable: persisted
SQL.correctAnswer: B) Atomic: all-or-nothing; Consistent: rules maintained; Isolated: concurrent safe; Durable: persisted
SQL.explanation: ACID ensures data integrity. Understanding isolation levels is critical for concurrent applications.

SQL.question: What is SQL injection and prevention?
options: B) Malicious SQL via user input — prevent with parameterized queries/prepared statements never string concat
SQL.correctAnswer: B) Malicious SQL via user input — prevent with parameterized queries/prepared statements never string concat
SQL.explanation: Never: query = 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ' + userId. Always use $1 placeholders.

SQL.question: What does EXPLAIN/EXPLAIN ANALYZE do?
options: B) Shows query execution plan — reveals full table scans, missing indexes, cost estimates
SQL.correctAnswer: B) Shows query execution plan — reveals full table scans, missing indexes, cost estimates
SQL.explanation: Always EXPLAIN slow queries before adding indexes. Seq Scan on large tables = add index.

SQL.question: What is the N+1 query problem in ORM context?
options: B) ORM fetching parent records then firing separate query per record for related data
SQL.correctAnswer: B) ORM fetching parent records then firing separate query per record for related data
SQL.explanation: Fix with eager loading (include/populate). Reduces N+1 queries to exactly 2.

DSA.question: What is Big O notation?
options: B) Describes worst-case time/space complexity growth rate as input size increases
DSA.correctAnswer: B) Describes worst-case time/space complexity growth rate as input size increases
DSA.explanation: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ) — memorize this order.

DSA.question: What is the two-pointer technique?
options: B) Two indices moving through array solving problems in O(n) instead of O(n²)
DSA.correctAnswer: B) Two indices moving through array solving problems in O(n) instead of O(n²)
DSA.explanation: Used for: palindrome check, container with most water, remove duplicates, sorted array sums.

DSA.question: What is the sliding window technique?
options: B) Maintain subarray window sliding through input to avoid recomputation — O(n) not O(n²)
DSA.correctAnswer: B) Maintain subarray window sliding through input to avoid recomputation — O(n) not O(n²)
DSA.explanation: Used for: max sum subarray, longest substring without repeat, minimum window substring.

DSA.question: What is Floyd's cycle detection algorithm?
options: B) Slow (1 step) and fast (2 steps) pointers — they meet if cycle exists in linked list
DSA.correctAnswer: B) Slow (1 step) and fast (2 steps) pointers — they meet if cycle exists in linked list
DSA.explanation: Also finds cycle start: when they meet, move one pointer to head, advance both 1 step to find entry.

DSA.question: What is a monotonic stack used for?
options: B) Stack maintaining monotonic order — used for next greater/smaller element in O(n)
DSA.correctAnswer: B) Stack maintaining monotonic order — used for next greater/smaller element in O(n)
DSA.explanation: Pattern: pop when current element violates monotonic property. Used in: daily temperatures, largest rectangle.

DSA.question: What is BST property?
options: B) Left subtree < root < right subtree — enables O(log n) search in balanced BST
DSA.correctAnswer: B) Left subtree < root < right subtree — enables O(log n) search in balanced BST
DSA.explanation: BST inorder traversal produces sorted sequence. Unbalanced BST degrades to O(n).

DSA.question: What is the difference between BFS and DFS?
options: B) BFS uses queue explores level-by-level; DFS uses stack/recursion explores depth-first
DSA.correctAnswer: B) BFS uses queue explores level-by-level; DFS uses stack/recursion explores depth-first
DSA.explanation: BFS for shortest path in unweighted graph. DFS for cycle detection, topological sort, connected components.

DSA.question: What is dynamic programming?
options: B) Optimization breaking problem into overlapping subproblems — memoization or tabulation
DSA.correctAnswer: B) Optimization breaking problem into overlapping subproblems — memoization or tabulation
DSA.explanation: DP identifies: optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems. Top-down=memoization, bottom-up=tabulation.

DSA.question: What is Dijkstra's algorithm?
options: B) Shortest path from source to all vertices in weighted non-negative graph using priority queue
DSA.correctAnswer: B) Shortest path from source to all vertices in weighted non-negative graph using priority queue
DSA.explanation: Time: O((V+E)log V) with binary heap. Fails with negative weights — use Bellman-Ford instead.

DSA.question: What is a trie (prefix tree)?
options: B) Tree storing strings character-by-character — O(L) insert/search where L is string length
DSA.correctAnswer: B) Tree storing strings character-by-character — O(L) insert/search where L is string length
DSA.explanation: Tries excel at: autocomplete, spell check, word search in grid, prefix matching.

TypeScript.question: What is TypeScript?
options: B) Typed superset of JavaScript — compiles to JS, adds static types caught at compile time
TypeScript.correctAnswer: B) Typed superset of JavaScript — compiles to JS, adds static types caught at compile time
TypeScript.explanation: TypeScript catches type errors before runtime — huge productivity boost in large codebases.

TypeScript.question: What is the difference between interface and type alias?
options: B) interface is extensible (extends, declaration merging); type can represent unions/intersections/primitives
C) type is for objects only
TypeScript.correctAnswer: B) interface is extensible (extends, declaration merging); type can represent unions/intersections/primitives
TypeScript.explanation: Use interface for object shapes (extensible). Use type for unions, intersections, complex types.

TypeScript.question: What is the unknown type vs any?
options: B) unknown is type-safe any — must narrow type before using; any bypasses all type checks
TypeScript.correctAnswer: B) unknown is type-safe any — must narrow type before using; any bypasses all type checks
TypeScript.explanation: Prefer unknown over any. Force yourself to handle all cases with type guards.

TypeScript.question: What is a union type?
options: B) Type that can be one of several types — string | number | boolean
TypeScript.correctAnswer: B) Type that can be one of several types — string | number | boolean
TypeScript.explanation: Use discriminated unions (type field) for exhaustive pattern matching with switch statements.

TypeScript.question: What is a generic type?
options: B) Parameterized type that works with multiple types while maintaining type safety: <T>
TypeScript.correctAnswer: B) Parameterized type that works with multiple types while maintaining type safety: <T>
TypeScript.explanation: Array<T>, Promise<T>, Record<K,V> are all generic. Create reusable type-safe utilities.

TypeScript.question: What is a discriminated union?
options: B) Union where each member has literal type field — enables exhaustive type narrowing
TypeScript.correctAnswer: B) Union where each member has literal type field — enables exhaustive type narrowing
TypeScript.explanation: type Shape = {kind: 'circle', r: number} | {kind: 'rect', w: number, h: number} — switch on kind.

TypeScript.question: What are utility types?
options: B) Built-in generic types: Partial<T>, Required<T>, Pick<T,K>, Omit<T,K>, Record<K,V> etc.
TypeScript.correctAnswer: B) Built-in generic types: Partial<T>, Required<T>, Pick<T,K>, Omit<T,K>, Record<K,V> etc.
TypeScript.explanation: Partial<User> makes all fields optional. Omit<User,'password'> removes sensitive fields.

TypeScript.question: What is the keyof operator?
options: B) Creates union type of all property names of type: keyof User = 'id' | 'name' | 'email'
TypeScript.correctAnswer: B) Creates union type of all property names of type: keyof User = 'id' | 'name' | 'email'
TypeScript.explanation: Combined with generics: function get<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K): T[K]

TypeScript.question: What is a conditional type?
options: B) Types that depend on condition: T extends string ? 'yes' : 'no'
TypeScript.correctAnswer: B) Types that depend on condition: T extends string ? 'yes' : 'no'
TypeScript.explanation: Enables type-level programming. NonNullable<T> = T extends null | undefined ? never : T

TypeScript.question: What is the infer keyword?
options: B) Extracts type within conditional type: T extends Promise<infer R> ? R : never
TypeScript.correctAnswer: B) Extracts type within conditional type: T extends Promise<infer R> ? R : never
TypeScript.explanation: Powerful for unwrapping types: ReturnType<T> = T extends (...args: any) => infer R ? R : any

Python.question: What is the GIL in Python?
options: B) Global Interpreter Lock — prevents multiple threads from executing Python bytecode simultaneously
Python.correctAnswer: B) Global Interpreter Lock — prevents multiple threads from executing Python bytecode simultaneously
Python.explanation: GIL means CPU-bound code can't be parallelized with threads. Use multiprocessing or asyncio instead.

Python.question: What is the difference between list and tuple?
options: B) Lists are mutable; tuples are immutable — tuples slightly faster and can be dict keys
D) Lists are ordered; tuples not
Python.correctAnswer: B) Lists are mutable; tuples are immutable — tuples slightly faster and can be dict keys
Python.explanation: Use tuples for data that shouldn't change (coordinates, RGB). Lists for collections that need modification.

Python.question: What is a Python decorator?
options: B) Function wrapping another function to add behavior — @syntax is syntactic sugar
Python.correctAnswer: B) Function wrapping another function to add behavior — @syntax is syntactic sugar
Python.explanation: @timer is equivalent to timer = timer_decorator(timer). Used for logging, auth, caching.

Python.question: What is a generator?
options: B) Function using yield returning values lazily one at a time — memory efficient for large sequences
Python.correctAnswer: B) Function using yield returning values lazily one at a time — memory efficient for large sequences
Python.explanation: Generators compute values on demand. Infinite sequences possible without memory issues.

Python.question: What is list comprehension vs generator expression?
options: B) [x for x in range] creates full list in memory; (x for x in range) generates lazily
Python.correctAnswer: B) [x for x in range] creates full list in memory; (x for x in range) generates lazily
Python.explanation: Use generator expressions when you only need to iterate once — memory efficient for large data.

Python.question: What is the difference between deep and shallow copy?
options: B) Shallow copies container, shares references to nested objects; deep copy recursively copies all
C) Deep is same but slower
D) Shallow always fails on nested
Python.correctAnswer: B) Shallow copies container, shares references to nested objects; deep copy recursively copies all
Python.explanation: Use copy.deepcopy() for deep. Shallow: list[:], dict.copy(), copy.copy(). Mutations affect original with shallow.

Python.question: What is *args and **kwargs?
options: B) *args collects positional args as tuple; **kwargs collects keyword args as dict
Python.correctAnswer: B) *args collects positional args as tuple; **kwargs collects keyword args as dict
Python.explanation: def f(*args, **kwargs) accepts any combination. Order: positional, *args, keyword, **kwargs.

Python.question: What is a context manager?
options: B) Protocol using __enter__/__exit__ ensuring resource cleanup — with statement
Python.correctAnswer: B) Protocol using __enter__/__exit__ ensuring resource cleanup — with statement
Python.explanation: with open('file') as f: — file always closed even if exception occurs. Use @contextmanager decorator.

Python.question: What is Python's MRO (Method Resolution Order)?
options: B) Order Python searches class hierarchy for methods — C3 linearization algorithm
Python.correctAnswer: B) Order Python searches class hierarchy for methods — C3 linearization algorithm
Python.explanation: Use ClassName.__mro__ to inspect. super() follows MRO enabling cooperative multiple inheritance.

Python.question: What is metaclass?
options: B) Class of a class — controls class creation behavior; type is default metaclass
Python.correctAnswer: B) Class of a class — controls class creation behavior; type is default metaclass
Python.explanation: class MyMeta(type): def __new__(mcs,name,bases,ns): ... — used in ORMs, frameworks.

Networking.question: What is the OSI model?
options: B) 7-layer framework: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
Networking.correctAnswer: B) 7-layer framework: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
Networking.explanation: Each layer has specific responsibility. Troubleshoot by layering: check physical before network.

Networking.question: What is the difference between TCP and UDP?
options: B) TCP: reliable ordered connection-based; UDP: fast unreliable connectionless
Networking.correctAnswer: B) TCP: reliable ordered connection-based; UDP: fast unreliable connectionless
Networking.explanation: Use TCP for: HTTP, SSH, databases. Use UDP for: video streaming, DNS, gaming.

Networking.question: What is the 3-way TCP handshake?
options: B) SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK establishes TCP connection before data transfer
Networking.correctAnswer: B) SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK establishes TCP connection before data transfer
Networking.explanation: Client sends SYN, server replies SYN-ACK, client sends ACK — connection established.

Networking.question: What is DNS and how it resolves?
options: B) Domain Name System: translates domain to IP — queries resolver, root, TLD, authoritative servers
Networking.correctAnswer: B) Domain Name System: translates domain to IP — queries resolver, root, TLD, authoritative servers
Networking.explanation: DNS hierarchy: Recursive resolver → Root nameserver → TLD (.com) → Authoritative → Returns IP.

Networking.question: What is HTTPS vs HTTP?
options: B) HTTP over TLS/SSL — data encrypted in transit, server identity verified with certificate
Networking.correctAnswer: B) HTTP over TLS/SSL — data encrypted in transit, server identity verified with certificate
Networking.explanation: TLS provides: confidentiality (encryption), integrity (MAC), authentication (certificates).

Networking.question: What is a CDN?
options: B) Distributed servers caching content near users — reduces latency and origin server load
Networking.correctAnswer: B) Distributed servers caching content near users — reduces latency and origin server load
Networking.explanation: CDN serves static assets from edge servers worldwide. Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront, Fastly.

Networking.question: What is a load balancer?
options: B) Distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers — horizontal scaling and high availability
Networking.correctAnswer: B) Distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers — horizontal scaling and high availability
Networking.explanation: Round-robin, least connections, IP hash algorithms. Layer 4 (TCP) vs Layer 7 (HTTP/content-aware).

Networking.question: What is WebSocket vs HTTP?
options: B) WebSocket: persistent full-duplex connection; HTTP: request-response, new connection each time
D) HTTP allows server push natively
Networking.correctAnswer: B) WebSocket: persistent full-duplex connection; HTTP: request-response, new connection each time
Networking.explanation: Use WebSocket for: chat, live updates, gaming. HTTP/2 server-sent events for one-way server push.

Networking.question: What is REST vs GraphQL?
options: B) REST: multiple endpoints each returning fixed data; GraphQL: single endpoint client specifies exact data
Networking.correctAnswer: B) REST: multiple endpoints each returning fixed data; GraphQL: single endpoint client specifies exact data
Networking.explanation: GraphQL solves over/under-fetching. REST simpler, better caching. Choose based on client diversity.

Networking.question: What is HTTP/2 vs HTTP/1.1?
options: B) HTTP/2: multiplexing (multiple requests one connection), server push, header compression
Networking.correctAnswer: B) HTTP/2: multiplexing (multiple requests one connection), server push, header compression
Networking.explanation: HTTP/1.1 suffers head-of-line blocking. HTTP/2 multiplexes — one TCP connection for all resources.

Cybersecurity.question: What is SQL injection?
options: B) Attacker inserts malicious SQL through input to manipulate queries — prevent with parameterized queries
Cybersecurity.correctAnswer: B) Attacker inserts malicious SQL through input to manipulate queries — prevent with parameterized queries
Cybersecurity.explanation: Never concatenate user input into SQL. Always use prepared statements with $1, $2 placeholders.

Cybersecurity.question: What is XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)?
options: B) Injecting malicious scripts into web pages viewed by users — reflected, stored, DOM-based
Cybersecurity.correctAnswer: B) Injecting malicious scripts into web pages viewed by users — reflected, stored, DOM-based
Cybersecurity.explanation: Prevent: escape HTML output, CSP headers, avoid innerHTML with user data, use textContent.

Cybersecurity.question: What is CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery)?
options: B) Tricks authenticated user's browser into sending unwanted requests to trusted site
Cybersecurity.correctAnswer: B) Tricks authenticated user's browser into sending unwanted requests to trusted site
Cybersecurity.explanation: Prevent with CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies, checking Origin/Referer headers.

Cybersecurity.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
options: B) Authentication: proving identity (who are you); Authorization: permissions (what can you do)
Cybersecurity.correctAnswer: B) Authentication: proving identity (who are you); Authorization: permissions (what can you do)
Cybersecurity.explanation: Login is authentication. Role-based access control (RBAC) is authorization.

Cybersecurity.question: What is symmetric vs asymmetric encryption?
options: B) Symmetric: same key encrypts/decrypts (AES); Asymmetric: public/private key pair (RSA, ECC)
Cybersecurity.correctAnswer: B) Symmetric: same key encrypts/decrypts (AES); Asymmetric: public/private key pair (RSA, ECC)
Cybersecurity.explanation: TLS uses asymmetric for key exchange, then symmetric (AES) for bulk data — best of both worlds.

Cybersecurity.question: What is a man-in-the-middle attack?
options: B) Attacker intercepts and potentially alters communication between two parties
Cybersecurity.correctAnswer: B) Attacker intercepts and potentially alters communication between two parties
Cybersecurity.explanation: HTTPS/certificate pinning prevents MITM. ARP poisoning enables MITM on local networks.

Cybersecurity.question: What is a zero-day vulnerability?
options: B) Unknown vulnerability with no patch available — exploited before vendor knows it exists
Cybersecurity.correctAnswer: B) Unknown vulnerability with no patch available — exploited before vendor knows it exists
Cybersecurity.explanation: High value targets (governments, large corps) face sophisticated zero-day attacks. Defense-in-depth critical.

Cybersecurity.question: What is OWASP Top 10?
options: B) Most critical web application security risks — Injection, Broken Auth, XSS, IDOR etc.
Cybersecurity.correctAnswer: B) Most critical web application security risks — Injection, Broken Auth, XSS, IDOR etc.
Cybersecurity.explanation: Current OWASP Top 10: Broken Access Control #1. Every developer should know this list.

Cybersecurity.question: What is a hash function in cryptography?
options: B) One-way function mapping arbitrary input to fixed-size digest — deterministic, collision resistant
Cybersecurity.correctAnswer: B) One-way function mapping arbitrary input to fixed-size digest — deterministic, collision resistant
Cybersecurity.explanation: SHA-256 for integrity. bcrypt/Argon2 for passwords — they're slow by design.

Cybersecurity.question: What is IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference)?
options: B) Accessing resources by manipulating ID in request without authorization check
Cybersecurity.correctAnswer: B) Accessing resources by manipulating ID in request without authorization check
Cybersecurity.explanation: /api/orders/123 — can user access 124? Always verify ownership server-side, not just authentication.

AI_ML.question: What is supervised vs unsupervised learning?
options: B) Supervised: labeled training data with targets; Unsupervised: finds patterns in unlabeled data
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Supervised: labeled training data with targets; Unsupervised: finds patterns in unlabeled data
AI_ML.explanation: Supervised: classification, regression. Unsupervised: clustering, dimensionality reduction, anomaly detection.

AI_ML.question: What is gradient descent?
options: B) Optimization algorithm iteratively adjusting parameters in direction of steepest loss decrease
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Optimization algorithm iteratively adjusting parameters in direction of steepest loss decrease
AI_ML.explanation: ∂Loss/∂weights tells slope. Move in opposite direction. Learning rate controls step size.

AI_ML.question: What is backpropagation?
options: B) Algorithm computing gradients layer-by-layer using chain rule — enables training deep networks
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Algorithm computing gradients layer-by-layer using chain rule — enables training deep networks
AI_ML.explanation: Forward pass computes output. Backward pass computes gradients. Optimizer updates weights.

AI_ML.question: What is overfitting vs underfitting?
options: B) Overfitting: memorizes training data fails on new; Underfitting: too simple fails on both
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Overfitting: memorizes training data fails on new; Underfitting: too simple fails on both
AI_ML.explanation: Fix overfitting: more data, dropout, regularization, simpler model. Fix underfitting: more features, complex model.

AI_ML.question: What is the transformer architecture?
options: B) Attention-based architecture using self-attention mechanisms — foundation of all modern LLMs
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Attention-based architecture using self-attention mechanisms — foundation of all modern LLMs
AI_ML.explanation: Self-attention allows attending to all positions simultaneously. Multi-head attention captures different relationships.

AI_ML.question: What is attention mechanism in transformers?
options: B) Query-Key-Value mechanism: attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k)V — each token attends to all others
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Query-Key-Value mechanism: attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k)V — each token attends to all others
AI_ML.explanation: √d_k scaling prevents dot products from getting too large in high dimensions.

AI_ML.question: What is transfer learning?
options: B) Using pre-trained model on new task — fine-tune existing weights rather than train from scratch
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Using pre-trained model on new task — fine-tune existing weights rather than train from scratch
AI_ML.explanation: GPT is pre-trained on internet, fine-tuned for specific tasks. Reduces data and compute requirements dramatically.

AI_ML.question: What is regularization?
options: B) Techniques reducing overfitting: L1/L2 add penalty to loss; dropout randomly zeros neurons
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Techniques reducing overfitting: L1/L2 add penalty to loss; dropout randomly zeros neurons
AI_ML.explanation: L1 (Lasso): sparsity, feature selection. L2 (Ridge): shrink weights. Dropout: ensemble effect.

AI_ML.question: What is batch normalization?
options: B) Normalizes layer inputs per mini-batch — reduces internal covariate shift, speeds training
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Normalizes layer inputs per mini-batch — reduces internal covariate shift, speeds training
AI_ML.explanation: Apply after linear layer, before activation. Allows higher learning rates, acts as regularizer.

AI_ML.question: What is the vanishing gradient problem?
options: B) Gradients become exponentially small in early layers — training stalls in deep networks
AI_ML.correctAnswer: B) Gradients become exponentially small in early layers — training stalls in deep networks
AI_ML.explanation: Solutions: ReLU activations, residual connections (ResNet), batch normalization, LSTM for sequences.

AgenticAI.question: What is an AI agent?
options: B) Autonomous system perceiving environment, making decisions, taking actions to achieve goals
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Autonomous system perceiving environment, making decisions, taking actions to achieve goals
AgenticAI.explanation: Agents combine LLMs with tools, memory, and planning to autonomously complete multi-step tasks.

AgenticAI.question: What is ReAct (Reasoning + Acting)?
options: B) Agent pattern interleaving reasoning traces and actions — LLM thinks then acts iteratively
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Agent pattern interleaving reasoning traces and actions — LLM thinks then acts iteratively
AgenticAI.explanation: Thought: I need to search X. Action: search(X). Observation: result. Thought: now I know... — iterative loop.

AgenticAI.question: What is tool calling / function calling in agents?
options: B) LLM outputs structured JSON describing function to call — framework executes and returns result
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) LLM outputs structured JSON describing function to call — framework executes and returns result
AgenticAI.explanation: LLM decides: {name: 'search', args: {query: 'weather NYC'}}. Framework calls search(), returns to LLM.

AgenticAI.question: What is agent memory architecture?
options: B) Short-term (context window), long-term (vector DB), episodic (past interactions), semantic (facts)
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Short-term (context window), long-term (vector DB), episodic (past interactions), semantic (facts)
AgenticAI.explanation: Short-term limited by context. Long-term via RAG retrieval. Episodic stores past conversations.

AgenticAI.question: What is the ReAct vs Plan-and-Execute pattern?
options: B) ReAct: interleaved think-act; Plan-and-Execute: plan entire sequence first, then execute steps
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) ReAct: interleaved think-act; Plan-and-Execute: plan entire sequence first, then execute steps
AgenticAI.explanation: Plan-and-Execute better for long tasks with clear steps. ReAct better for exploratory/adaptive tasks.

AgenticAI.question: What is LangChain?
options: B) Framework for building LLM applications — chains, agents, tools, memory, RAG abstractions
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Framework for building LLM applications — chains, agents, tools, memory, RAG abstractions
AgenticAI.explanation: LangChain provides standardized interfaces for LLMs, vector stores, tools enabling complex pipelines.

AgenticAI.question: What is the difference between chain and agent in LangChain?
options: B) Chain: fixed predetermined steps; Agent: LLM dynamically decides which tools to use
D) Chain uses LLM; Agent doesn't
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Chain: fixed predetermined steps; Agent: LLM dynamically decides which tools to use
AgenticAI.explanation: Use chains for predictable workflows. Use agents when decision logic is complex or dynamic.

AgenticAI.question: What is a multi-agent system?
options: B) Multiple specialized agents collaborating — each has specific role, communicate to solve complex tasks
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Multiple specialized agents collaborating — each has specific role, communicate to solve complex tasks
AgenticAI.explanation: AutoGen, CrewAI patterns: orchestrator delegates to specialist agents (researcher, coder, reviewer).

AgenticAI.question: What is agent hallucination and mitigation?
options: B) Agent confidently takes wrong action — mitigate with tool verification, human-in-loop, self-reflection
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Agent confidently takes wrong action — mitigate with tool verification, human-in-loop, self-reflection
AgenticAI.explanation: Grounding via tools, retrieval, structured outputs reduce hallucination. Human approval for irreversible actions.

AgenticAI.question: What is a tool in agent context?
options: B) Function agent can call to interact with world — web search, code execution, DB queries, APIs
AgenticAI.correctAnswer: B) Function agent can call to interact with world — web search, code execution, DB queries, APIs
AgenticAI.explanation: Tools are agent's hands. Careful tool design with clear descriptions improves LLM tool selection accuracy.

beginner.question: What does HTML stand for?
beginner.correctAnswer: A) Hyper Text Markup Language
beginner.explanation: HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language — the standard language for creating web pages.

beginner.question: Which tag creates a hyperlink?
beginner.correctAnswer: B) <a>
beginner.explanation: The <a> (anchor) tag with href attribute creates hyperlinks.

beginner.question: What is the correct HTML for the largest heading?
beginner.explanation: <h1> is the largest heading tag, <h6> is the smallest.

beginner.question: Which HTML attribute specifies an alternate text for an image?
beginner.correctAnswer: C) alt
beginner.explanation: The alt attribute provides alternative text for screen readers and when image fails to load.

beginner.question: What does the DOCTYPE declaration do?
options: B) Tells browser which HTML version is being used
beginner.correctAnswer: B) Tells browser which HTML version is being used
beginner.explanation: <!DOCTYPE html> tells the browser to use HTML5 rendering mode.

beginner.question: Which tag is used for an unordered list?
beginner.correctAnswer: C) <ul>
beginner.explanation: <ul> creates unordered (bulleted) lists, <ol> creates ordered (numbered) lists.

beginner.question: Which attribute makes an input field required?
beginner.correctAnswer: C) required
beginner.explanation: The required attribute prevents form submission if the field is empty.

beginner.question: What is the difference between id and class attributes?
options: B) id must be unique per page; class can be reused on multiple elements
C) class must be unique; id can repeat
D) id is for CSS; class is for JavaScript
beginner.correctAnswer: B) id must be unique per page; class can be reused on multiple elements
beginner.explanation: Multiple elements can share a class but each id must be unique within a document.

intermediate.question: What is semantic HTML?
options: B) Using HTML elements that convey meaning about content structure
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Using HTML elements that convey meaning about content structure
intermediate.explanation: Semantic elements like <article>, <nav>, <section> describe their content, improving accessibility and SEO.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between <div> and <span>?
options: B) div is block-level; span is inline
C) span is block-level; div is inline
D) div is for text; span is for images
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) div is block-level; span is inline
intermediate.explanation: Block elements start on new line and take full width. Inline elements flow within text.

intermediate.question: What does the <meta charset='UTF-8'> tag do?
options: B) Defines character encoding for the document
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Defines character encoding for the document
intermediate.explanation: UTF-8 encoding supports virtually all characters and is the standard for web pages.

intermediate.question: Which HTML5 element defines navigation links?
intermediate.correctAnswer: C) <nav>
intermediate.explanation: <nav> is a semantic element specifically for navigation menus and links.

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of the <figure> element?
options: B) Self-contained content like images/diagrams with optional caption
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Self-contained content like images/diagrams with optional caption
intermediate.explanation: <figure> with <figcaption> provides semantic grouping for media content with descriptions.

advanced.question: What is ARIA in HTML?
options: B) Accessible Rich Internet Applications — attributes improving accessibility
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Accessible Rich Internet Applications — attributes improving accessibility
advanced.explanation: ARIA attributes like role, aria-label, aria-hidden help screen readers understand page structure.

advanced.question: What does the async attribute do on a <script> tag?
options: A) Runs script after page loads
B) Script downloads asynchronously and executes immediately when ready — doesn't block parsing
C) Makes script run in worker thread
D) Defers script to end of body
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Script downloads asynchronously and executes immediately when ready — doesn't block parsing
advanced.explanation: async downloads script without blocking, executes when ready. defer waits for HTML parsing to complete.

advanced.question: What is the difference between defer and async script loading?
options: B) async executes as soon as downloaded; defer waits for DOM parsing to complete before executing
advanced.correctAnswer: B) async executes as soon as downloaded; defer waits for DOM parsing to complete before executing
advanced.explanation: Use defer for scripts that depend on DOM. Use async for independent scripts like analytics.

advanced.question: What is Content Security Policy (CSP)?
options: B) HTTP header controlling what resources browser can load — prevents XSS attacks
advanced.correctAnswer: B) HTTP header controlling what resources browser can load — prevents XSS attacks
advanced.explanation: CSP via meta tag or HTTP header restricts sources of scripts, styles, images to prevent injection attacks.

advanced.question: What does the <template> element do?
options: A) Creates HTML templates for emails
B) Holds client-side HTML not rendered until activated via JavaScript
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Holds client-side HTML not rendered until activated via JavaScript
advanced.explanation: <template> content is inert — not rendered, no scripts run — until cloned into DOM via JS.

advanced.question: What is the Shadow DOM?
options: B) Encapsulated DOM subtree for Web Components — styles and scripts don't leak in/out
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Encapsulated DOM subtree for Web Components — styles and scripts don't leak in/out
advanced.explanation: Shadow DOM provides encapsulation for custom elements, preventing style conflicts.

advanced.question: What is a Web Component?
options: B) Suite of browser APIs to create custom, reusable HTML elements — Custom Elements + Shadow DOM + Templates
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Suite of browser APIs to create custom, reusable HTML elements — Custom Elements + Shadow DOM + Templates
advanced.explanation: Web Components are framework-agnostic reusable elements using native browser APIs.

beginner.question: What is the CSS box model?
options: B) content + padding + border + margin — defines element's space and dimensions
beginner.correctAnswer: B) content + padding + border + margin — defines element's space and dimensions
beginner.explanation: Every element is a box. Understanding content/padding/border/margin is fundamental to layout.

beginner.question: What is the difference between visibility:hidden and display:none?
options: B) visibility:hidden hides but keeps space; display:none removes element and collapses space
beginner.correctAnswer: B) visibility:hidden hides but keeps space; display:none removes element and collapses space
beginner.explanation: visibility:hidden is like making element transparent. display:none removes it from layout completely.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between box-sizing: content-box vs border-box?
options: B) content-box: width=content only; border-box: width includes padding+border
D) content-box is default in modern browsers
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) content-box: width=content only; border-box: width includes padding+border
intermediate.explanation: border-box makes sizing intuitive — a 200px box is always 200px regardless of padding/border.

intermediate.question: What is CSS specificity order (highest to lowest)?
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) inline > ID > class/pseudo-class > element
intermediate.explanation: Inline styles override all. ID selectors > class selectors > element selectors.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between Flexbox and Grid?
options: B) Flexbox is 1D (row or column); Grid is 2D (rows AND columns simultaneously)
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Flexbox is 1D (row or column); Grid is 2D (rows AND columns simultaneously)
intermediate.explanation: Use Flexbox for single-axis layouts. Use Grid for complex 2D page layouts.

intermediate.question: What does position: sticky do?
options: B) Scrolls normally until hitting threshold, then sticks in place
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Scrolls normally until hitting threshold, then sticks in place
intermediate.explanation: Sticky is a hybrid of relative and fixed — sticks within its scroll container.

intermediate.question: What is a CSS pseudo-element?
options: B) Keywords like ::before, ::after that style specific parts of element
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Keywords like ::before, ::after that style specific parts of element
intermediate.explanation: ::before and ::after inject content before/after element. ::first-line styles first line of text.

intermediate.question: What is z-index and what does it require?
options: B) Controls stacking order — only works on positioned elements (not static)
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Controls stacking order — only works on positioned elements (not static)
intermediate.explanation: For z-index to work, element must have position: relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky.

intermediate.question: What is a CSS custom property (variable)?
options: B) --name: value variables accessed with var(--name), scoped to selector
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) --name: value variables accessed with var(--name), scoped to selector
intermediate.explanation: CSS variables enable theming, are reactive to changes, and cascade through the DOM.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between em and rem units?
options: B) em relative to parent's font-size; rem relative to root (html) font-size
C) rem is relative to parent
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) em relative to parent's font-size; rem relative to root (html) font-size
intermediate.explanation: rem is more predictable for font-sizes as it doesn't compound with nested elements.

intermediate.question: What is CSS transform vs transition?
options: B) transform changes geometry (rotate/scale/translate); transition animates property change over time
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) transform changes geometry (rotate/scale/translate); transition animates property change over time
intermediate.explanation: Combine both: use transform for what to change, transition for how long the change takes.

intermediate.question: What is CSS Grid fr unit?
options: B) Fractional unit — represents fraction of available space in grid container
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Fractional unit — represents fraction of available space in grid container
intermediate.explanation: 1fr 2fr gives columns ratio 1:2 of available space after fixed columns are allocated.

intermediate.question: What is BEM naming methodology?
options: B) Block__Element--Modifier — naming convention preventing style conflicts
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Block__Element--Modifier — naming convention preventing style conflicts
intermediate.explanation: BEM creates predictable, maintainable CSS by defining clear naming relationships.

intermediate.question: What is the cascade in CSS?
options: B) Algorithm determining which CSS rule applies when multiple rules target same element
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Algorithm determining which CSS rule applies when multiple rules target same element
intermediate.explanation: Cascade considers specificity, source order, importance, and inheritance to resolve conflicts.

advanced.question: What does will-change property do?
options: B) Hints browser about upcoming changes — allows GPU layer optimization in advance
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Hints browser about upcoming changes — allows GPU layer optimization in advance
advanced.explanation: Use will-change: transform on animated elements to trigger GPU compositing layer.

advanced.question: What is CSS containment (contain property)?
options: B) Tells browser element is independent — enables performance optimizations by limiting reflow scope
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Tells browser element is independent — enables performance optimizations by limiting reflow scope
advanced.explanation: contain: layout|style|paint|size tells browser changes inside don't affect outside.

advanced.question: What is CSS Grid auto-fit vs auto-fill?
options: B) auto-fill keeps empty tracks; auto-fit collapses empty tracks — both with minmax() create responsive grids
C) auto-fit is for images only
advanced.correctAnswer: B) auto-fill keeps empty tracks; auto-fit collapses empty tracks — both with minmax() create responsive grids
advanced.explanation: grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(200px, 1fr)) creates responsive layouts without media queries.

advanced.question: What is CSS logical properties?
options: B) Writing-mode independent properties: margin-inline instead of margin-left/right
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Writing-mode independent properties: margin-inline instead of margin-left/right
advanced.explanation: Logical properties adapt to writing direction automatically, better for international/RTL layouts.

advanced.question: What is the @layer rule?
options: B) Defines cascade layers to control specificity without specificity wars
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Defines cascade layers to control specificity without specificity wars
advanced.explanation: @layer lets you organize styles into layers where earlier layers have lower specificity.

advanced.question: What is CSS Houdini?
options: B) Low-level CSS APIs giving developers direct access to CSS rendering engine
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Low-level CSS APIs giving developers direct access to CSS rendering engine
advanced.explanation: Houdini APIs like Paint API, Layout API let developers extend CSS itself with JavaScript.

beginner.question: What is the output of typeof null?
beginner.correctAnswer: B) object
beginner.explanation: This is a famous JS bug. typeof null returns 'object' — has been kept for backward compatibility.

beginner.question: What is the difference between let, const, and var?
options: B) var is function-scoped hoisted; let/const are block-scoped; const can't be reassigned
beginner.correctAnswer: B) var is function-scoped hoisted; let/const are block-scoped; const can't be reassigned
beginner.explanation: Prefer const by default, let when reassignment needed, avoid var due to hoisting confusion.

beginner.question: What is the difference between == and ===?
options: B) == coerces types then compares; === compares value AND type without coercion
beginner.correctAnswer: B) == coerces types then compares; === compares value AND type without coercion
beginner.explanation: Always use === to avoid subtle bugs from type coercion.

intermediate.question: What is a closure?
options: B) Function retaining access to outer scope variables even after outer function returns
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Function retaining access to outer scope variables even after outer function returns
intermediate.explanation: Closures enable data encapsulation, module patterns, and callbacks with preserved context.

intermediate.question: What is event delegation?
options: B) Adding single listener to parent to handle events from all children via bubbling
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Adding single listener to parent to handle events from all children via bubbling
intermediate.explanation: Event delegation improves performance for dynamic lists — one handler instead of hundreds.

intermediate.question: What is the prototype chain?
options: B) Mechanism where objects inherit properties from their prototype object up the chain to null
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Mechanism where objects inherit properties from their prototype object up the chain to null
