intermediate.explanation: When accessing property, JS looks up prototype chain until found or reaches null.

intermediate.question: What is the event loop?
options: B) Single-threaded mechanism: call stack + web APIs + callback queue + microtask queue
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Single-threaded mechanism: call stack + web APIs + callback queue + microtask queue
intermediate.explanation: Event loop enables non-blocking I/O by offloading to Web APIs and processing callbacks.

intermediate.question: What does async/await compile to?
options: B) Syntactic sugar over Promises — async returns Promise, await pauses execution until resolved
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Syntactic sugar over Promises — async returns Promise, await pauses execution until resolved
intermediate.explanation: async/await makes async code readable. Under the hood, it still uses Promises.

intermediate.question: What is memoization?
options: B) Optimization caching function results — same inputs return cached output avoiding recomputation
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Optimization caching function results — same inputs return cached output avoiding recomputation
intermediate.explanation: Useful for expensive pure functions called repeatedly with same arguments.

intermediate.question: What is a pure function?
options: B) Always produces same output for same input AND has zero side effects
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Always produces same output for same input AND has zero side effects
intermediate.explanation: Pure functions are predictable, testable, and composable — cornerstone of functional programming.

intermediate.question: What is the temporal dead zone?
options: B) Period where let/const is declared but not initialized — accessing throws ReferenceError
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Period where let/const is declared but not initialized — accessing throws ReferenceError
intermediate.explanation: Unlike var (initialized to undefined), let/const throw if accessed before declaration.

intermediate.question: What does Object.freeze() do?
options: B) Prevents adding/removing/modifying own properties — shallow freeze only
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Prevents adding/removing/modifying own properties — shallow freeze only
intermediate.explanation: Freeze is shallow — nested objects are still mutable. For deep immutability, recurse manually.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between deep and shallow clone?
options: B) Shallow clone copies top-level properties with shared references; deep clone recursively copies all nested objects
C) Deep is slower but same result
D) Shallow creates new object always
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Shallow clone copies top-level properties with shared references; deep clone recursively copies all nested objects
intermediate.explanation: Use structuredClone() for deep clone, spread operator {...obj} for shallow clone.

advanced.question: Microtasks vs macrotasks — execution order?
options: B) After each task: ALL microtasks drain first (Promises), then one macrotask (setTimeout)
advanced.correctAnswer: B) After each task: ALL microtasks drain first (Promises), then one macrotask (setTimeout)
advanced.explanation: Promise.then callbacks are microtasks — they run before setTimeout callbacks always.

advanced.question: What is currying?
options: B) Transforming f(a,b,c) into f(a)(b)(c) — each call takes one arg and returns function
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Transforming f(a,b,c) into f(a)(b)(c) — each call takes one arg and returns function
advanced.explanation: Currying enables partial application — pre-fill some arguments and reuse the specialized function.

advanced.question: What is the Proxy object?
options: B) Wraps target object intercepting fundamental operations (get/set/delete) via handlers
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Wraps target object intercepting fundamental operations (get/set/delete) via handlers
advanced.explanation: Proxies power reactive frameworks (Vue 3), validation, and object virtualization.

advanced.question: What is WeakMap vs Map?
options: B) WeakMap keys must be objects, weakly held — garbage collected when no other reference exists
D) WeakMap can't iterate (same as Map)
advanced.correctAnswer: B) WeakMap keys must be objects, weakly held — garbage collected when no other reference exists
advanced.explanation: WeakMap prevents memory leaks for object metadata. Can't be iterated or checked for size.

advanced.question: What is tail call optimization?
options: B) Reusing current stack frame when last action is recursive call — prevents stack overflow
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Reusing current stack frame when last action is recursive call — prevents stack overflow
advanced.explanation: TCO allows infinite recursion without stack overflow if recursive call is in tail position.

advanced.question: What is a generator function?
options: B) Function using yield to pause/resume — returns iterator producing values lazily
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Function using yield to pause/resume — returns iterator producing values lazily
advanced.explanation: Generators enable infinite sequences, custom iterators, and cooperative multitasking.

advanced.question: What is Symbol.iterator?
options: B) Well-known symbol defining custom iteration — makes object work with for...of and spread
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Well-known symbol defining custom iteration — makes object work with for...of and spread
advanced.explanation: Implement [Symbol.iterator]() to make any object iterable with for...of loops.

beginner.question: What is Node.js?
options: B) JavaScript runtime built on V8 enabling server-side execution with non-blocking I/O
beginner.correctAnswer: B) JavaScript runtime built on V8 enabling server-side execution with non-blocking I/O
beginner.explanation: Node's event-driven, non-blocking model makes it efficient for I/O-heavy applications.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between require() and import?
options: B) require() is CommonJS synchronous dynamic; import is ES modules static async tree-shakeable
D) require() is for JSON only
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) require() is CommonJS synchronous dynamic; import is ES modules static async tree-shakeable
intermediate.explanation: Use .mjs or 'type':'module' in package.json for ES modules in Node.

intermediate.question: What is the EventEmitter?
options: B) Core class for event-driven architecture — on() to subscribe, emit() to trigger events
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Core class for event-driven architecture — on() to subscribe, emit() to trigger events
intermediate.explanation: Most Node.js core modules extend EventEmitter — streams, HTTP server, etc.

intermediate.question: What is a Node.js stream?
options: B) Abstract interface for reading/writing data in chunks — memory efficient for large files
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Abstract interface for reading/writing data in chunks — memory efficient for large files
intermediate.explanation: 4 types: Readable, Writable, Duplex, Transform. Pipe them for efficient data processing.

intermediate.question: What is middleware in Express?
options: B) Function(req, res, next) executing between request and route handler
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Function(req, res, next) executing between request and route handler
intermediate.explanation: Middleware chain processes request: authentication, logging, parsing, validation before handler.

intermediate.question: What is JWT and its structure?
options: B) JSON Web Token: header.payload.signature — stateless authentication token
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) JSON Web Token: header.payload.signature — stateless authentication token
intermediate.explanation: JWT is signed (not encrypted by default). Never store sensitive data in payload — it's base64 decoded easily.

intermediate.question: What is bcrypt and why use it for passwords?
options: B) Password hashing with salt — deliberately slow to prevent brute force attacks
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Password hashing with salt — deliberately slow to prevent brute force attacks
intermediate.explanation: bcrypt's cost factor makes it exponentially slower per guess — 12 rounds is standard.

intermediate.question: What is CORS and how to handle it in Node?
options: B) Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — browser security policy; handled with cors middleware
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — browser security policy; handled with cors middleware
intermediate.explanation: CORS headers tell browser which origins can access your API. Configure origin whitelist carefully.

intermediate.question: What is rate limiting?
options: B) Restricting requests per IP/user in time window — prevents abuse and DoS attacks
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Restricting requests per IP/user in time window — prevents abuse and DoS attacks
intermediate.explanation: Use express-rate-limit to apply per-route or global limits, optionally backed by Redis.

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of helmet.js?
options: B) Express middleware setting HTTP security headers — X-Frame-Options, CSP, HSTS etc.
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Express middleware setting HTTP security headers — X-Frame-Options, CSP, HSTS etc.
intermediate.explanation: Helmet sets ~11 security headers by default preventing clickjacking, MIME sniffing, XSS.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous in Node context?
options: B) Sync blocks event loop preventing other operations; async offloads via callbacks/promises
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Sync blocks event loop preventing other operations; async offloads via callbacks/promises
intermediate.explanation: Never use sync file/network operations in production Node servers — they block all other requests.

intermediate.question: What is PM2?
options: B) Production process manager — clustering, zero-downtime reload, monitoring, log management
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Production process manager — clustering, zero-downtime reload, monitoring, log management
intermediate.explanation: PM2 keeps Node apps alive, automatically restarts on crash, manages multiple app instances.

intermediate.question: What is __dirname vs process.cwd()?
options: B) __dirname is directory of current module file; process.cwd() is current working directory when node started
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) __dirname is directory of current module file; process.cwd() is current working directory when node started
intermediate.explanation: Use __dirname for paths relative to module file. process.cwd() changes based on where node is run from.

advanced.question: What is the cluster module?
options: B) Creates child processes sharing server port — utilizes multiple CPU cores
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Creates child processes sharing server port — utilizes multiple CPU cores
advanced.explanation: Since Node is single-threaded, cluster module spawns workers equal to CPU cores for load balancing.

advanced.question: What is libuv in Node.js?
options: B) C library providing async I/O, event loop, and thread pool to Node.js
advanced.correctAnswer: B) C library providing async I/O, event loop, and thread pool to Node.js
advanced.explanation: libuv's thread pool handles file system ops and DNS that can't be done asynchronously by OS.

advanced.question: What is the difference between process.nextTick() and setImmediate()?
options: B) nextTick runs before I/O callbacks (same phase); setImmediate runs in check phase after I/O
advanced.correctAnswer: B) nextTick runs before I/O callbacks (same phase); setImmediate runs in check phase after I/O
advanced.explanation: Use nextTick for critical callbacks. Use setImmediate to yield to I/O callbacks first.

advanced.question: What is graceful shutdown in Node.js?
options: B) Listening for SIGTERM to finish active requests before stopping — prevents data loss
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Listening for SIGTERM to finish active requests before stopping — prevents data loss
advanced.explanation: Handle SIGTERM/SIGINT signals. Stop accepting new connections. Wait for active requests to complete.

advanced.question: What is a Worker Thread in Node.js?
options: B) True parallel thread for CPU-intensive tasks — unlike cluster, shares memory
advanced.correctAnswer: B) True parallel thread for CPU-intensive tasks — unlike cluster, shares memory
advanced.explanation: Use Worker Threads for CPU-bound work (image processing, crypto). Use cluster for HTTP scaling.

advanced.question: What is connection pooling in databases?
options: B) Pre-created reusable connections — avoids expensive connection setup per request
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Pre-created reusable connections — avoids expensive connection setup per request
advanced.explanation: Mongoose maintains connection pool automatically. Configure poolSize based on expected concurrency.

advanced.question: What is the N+1 query problem?
options: B) Fetching N parent records then N separate queries for children — O(N+1) total queries
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Fetching N parent records then N separate queries for children — O(N+1) total queries
advanced.explanation: Fix with populate() in Mongoose or JOIN in SQL — reduces to 2 queries regardless of N.

beginner.question: What is the Virtual DOM?
options: B) In-memory JS representation of real DOM — React diffs it to minimize actual DOM mutations
beginner.correctAnswer: B) In-memory JS representation of real DOM — React diffs it to minimize actual DOM mutations
beginner.explanation: Virtual DOM enables declarative programming — React handles efficient DOM updates automatically.

intermediate.question: What triggers a React re-render?
options: B) State change, prop change, parent re-render, or context change
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) State change, prop change, parent re-render, or context change
intermediate.explanation: Understanding what causes re-renders is key to React performance optimization.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between useState and useRef?
options: B) useState triggers re-render on update; useRef persists value without triggering re-render
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) useState triggers re-render on update; useRef persists value without triggering re-render
intermediate.explanation: Use useRef for DOM references, timers, previous values — anything that shouldn't cause re-render.

intermediate.question: What does empty [] dependency array in useEffect mean?
options: B) Runs only once after initial mount — like componentDidMount
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Runs only once after initial mount — like componentDidMount
intermediate.explanation: No deps = run once. Deps array = run when deps change. No array = run every render.

intermediate.question: What is useMemo vs useCallback?
options: B) useMemo memoizes computed VALUE; useCallback memoizes FUNCTION reference
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) useMemo memoizes computed VALUE; useCallback memoizes FUNCTION reference
intermediate.explanation: Use useMemo for expensive calculations. Use useCallback to stabilize function identity passed as props.

intermediate.question: What is React reconciliation?
options: B) Diffing algorithm comparing old vs new virtual DOM to determine minimal real DOM updates
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Diffing algorithm comparing old vs new virtual DOM to determine minimal real DOM updates
intermediate.explanation: React's O(n) reconciliation algorithm uses element type and key prop to efficiently update DOM.

intermediate.question: What is React.memo()?
options: B) HOC memoizing component — skips re-render if props haven't changed (shallow comparison)
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) HOC memoizing component — skips re-render if props haven't changed (shallow comparison)
intermediate.explanation: React.memo prevents re-renders when parent re-renders but props are unchanged.

intermediate.question: What is useReducer vs useState?
options: B) useReducer better for complex state with multiple sub-values or next state depending on previous
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) useReducer better for complex state with multiple sub-values or next state depending on previous
intermediate.explanation: When state transitions are complex (like Redux), useReducer with dispatch/action is cleaner.

intermediate.question: What is Context API used for?
options: B) Passing data through component tree without prop drilling — global state solution
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Passing data through component tree without prop drilling — global state solution
intermediate.explanation: Context is great for theme, auth, language. For complex state, consider Zustand or Redux.

intermediate.question: What is a controlled vs uncontrolled component?
options: B) Controlled: form value managed by React state; Uncontrolled: form value managed by DOM ref
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Controlled: form value managed by React state; Uncontrolled: form value managed by DOM ref
intermediate.explanation: Controlled components give React full control over form data. Uncontrolled uses ref.current.value.

intermediate.question: What is React StrictMode?
options: B) Dev-only wrapper double-invoking functions to detect side effects and deprecated APIs
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Dev-only wrapper double-invoking functions to detect side effects and deprecated APIs
intermediate.explanation: StrictMode intentionally double-renders to expose impure render functions and effect cleanup bugs.

intermediate.question: What is the key prop and why must it be stable?
options: B) Helps React identify list items — unstable keys (index) cause bugs when list reorders
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Helps React identify list items — unstable keys (index) cause bugs when list reorders
intermediate.explanation: Never use array index as key for reorderable lists — use unique IDs from data.

advanced.question: What is the stale closure problem?
options: B) Hook captures outdated value from previous render — fixed by adding to dependency array
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Hook captures outdated value from previous render — fixed by adding to dependency array
advanced.explanation: Classic bug: setInterval inside useEffect with empty deps reads stale state forever.

advanced.question: What is React.lazy() and Suspense?
options: B) Code splitting: lazy() dynamically imports component; Suspense shows fallback while loading
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Code splitting: lazy() dynamically imports component; Suspense shows fallback while loading
advanced.explanation: Reduces initial bundle size by loading components only when needed.

advanced.question: What is a Higher Order Component (HOC)?
options: B) Function taking component and returning enhanced component — pattern for reusing logic
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Function taking component and returning enhanced component — pattern for reusing logic
advanced.explanation: HOCs like withAuth, withTheme add cross-cutting concerns. Hooks largely replaced HOCs.

advanced.question: What is Concurrent React?
options: B) React 18 feature enabling interruptible rendering — can pause/resume based on priority
advanced.correctAnswer: B) React 18 feature enabling interruptible rendering — can pause/resume based on priority
advanced.explanation: Concurrent features: automatic batching, useTransition, useDeferredValue, Suspense for data.

advanced.question: What is useTransition hook?
options: B) Marks state update as non-urgent — keeps UI responsive during expensive re-renders
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Marks state update as non-urgent — keeps UI responsive during expensive re-renders
advanced.explanation: Wrap slow state updates with startTransition to prevent blocking urgent updates (typing).

advanced.question: What is the render prop pattern?
options: B) Sharing logic via prop whose value is a function returning JSX
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Sharing logic via prop whose value is a function returning JSX
advanced.explanation: <Component render={(data) => <UI data={data}/>}/> — hooks largely replaced this pattern.

advanced.question: What is React Portal?
options: B) Renders children into different DOM node outside parent hierarchy — for modals/tooltips
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Renders children into different DOM node outside parent hierarchy — for modals/tooltips
advanced.explanation: ReactDOM.createPortal(child, container) renders in document.body while staying in React tree.

advanced.question: What is forwardRef?
options: B) Allows function component to forward its ref to child DOM element or component
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Allows function component to forward its ref to child DOM element or component
advanced.explanation: Essential for component libraries exposing imperative handles to consumers.

beginner.question: What is INNER JOIN vs LEFT JOIN?
options: B) INNER: only matching rows; LEFT: all left rows plus matching right (NULL if no match)
C) LEFT only returns left table
beginner.correctAnswer: B) INNER: only matching rows; LEFT: all left rows plus matching right (NULL if no match)
beginner.explanation: LEFT JOIN is most common — use to include records even when related record doesn't exist.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?

C) HAVING filters before GROUP BY
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY
intermediate.explanation: WHERE can't use aggregate functions. HAVING is specifically for filtering aggregated results.

intermediate.question: What is a database index and trade-offs?

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Data structure speeding up reads — trade-off: slower writes and inserts, more storage
intermediate.explanation: Index every WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY column. Too many indexes slow INSERT/UPDATE significantly.

intermediate.question: What is database normalization?

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Organizing tables eliminating redundancy — 1NF atomic, 2NF no partial deps, 3NF no transitive deps
intermediate.explanation: Normalize to 3NF typically. Denormalize selectively for read performance when needed.

intermediate.question: What is a CTE (Common Table Expression)?

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Named temporary result with WITH clause — makes complex queries readable and reusable in query
intermediate.explanation: CTEs can reference themselves (recursive CTEs) for hierarchical data like org charts.

intermediate.question: What is a transaction and ACID?
options: B) Atomic: all-or-nothing; Consistent: rules maintained; Isolated: concurrent safe; Durable: persisted
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Atomic: all-or-nothing; Consistent: rules maintained; Isolated: concurrent safe; Durable: persisted
intermediate.explanation: ACID ensures data integrity. Understanding isolation levels is critical for concurrent applications.

intermediate.question: What is SQL injection and prevention?

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Malicious SQL via user input — prevent with parameterized queries/prepared statements never string concat
intermediate.explanation: Never: query = 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ' + userId. Always use $1 placeholders.

intermediate.question: What is COALESCE() function?

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Returns first non-NULL value from arguments — essential for NULL handling
intermediate.explanation: COALESCE(phone, email, 'N/A') — returns first non-null value, great for fallback values.

intermediate.question: What is UNION vs UNION ALL?

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) UNION deduplicates (slower sort); UNION ALL keeps all rows including duplicates (faster)
intermediate.explanation: Prefer UNION ALL when you know results won't have duplicates — avoids expensive dedup sort.

intermediate.question: What is a stored procedure?

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Pre-compiled SQL code stored in database — reusable, can contain logic and transactions
intermediate.explanation: Stored procedures reduce network round-trips but move business logic to DB — controversial approach.

advanced.question: What is a window function?

advanced.correctAnswer: B) Calculation across related rows without collapsing them — ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, SUM OVER
advanced.explanation: OVER(PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary) calculates within groups while keeping all rows.

advanced.question: What does EXPLAIN/EXPLAIN ANALYZE do?

advanced.correctAnswer: B) Shows query execution plan — reveals full table scans, missing indexes, cost estimates
advanced.explanation: Always EXPLAIN slow queries before adding indexes. Seq Scan on large tables = add index.

advanced.question: What is the N+1 query problem in ORM context?
options: B) ORM fetching parent records then firing separate query per record for related data
advanced.correctAnswer: B) ORM fetching parent records then firing separate query per record for related data
advanced.explanation: Fix with eager loading (include/populate). Reduces N+1 queries to exactly 2.

advanced.question: What is database sharding?
options: B) Horizontal partitioning distributing data across multiple DB instances by shard key
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Horizontal partitioning distributing data across multiple DB instances by shard key
advanced.explanation: Shard by user_id, geography, etc. Adds complexity — exhaust vertical scaling first.

advanced.question: What is a composite index and column order?

advanced.correctAnswer: B) Index on multiple columns — queries must match leftmost columns for index to be used
advanced.explanation: Index (first_name, last_name) helps queries on first_name but not last_name alone.

advanced.question: What is optimistic vs pessimistic locking?
options: B) Optimistic: check at save (version column); Pessimistic: lock row immediately on read
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Optimistic: check at save (version column); Pessimistic: lock row immediately on read
advanced.explanation: Optimistic better for low-conflict systems. Pessimistic for high-contention critical sections.

advanced.question: What is database denormalization?
options: B) Intentionally adding redundancy to improve read performance — trade-off vs update anomalies
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Intentionally adding redundancy to improve read performance — trade-off vs update anomalies
advanced.explanation: Cache computed values, store duplicated data when read >> write and joins are expensive.

advanced.question: What is a materialized view?

advanced.correctAnswer: B) Pre-computed query result stored as physical table — fast reads, needs refresh on data change
advanced.explanation: Great for expensive aggregations queried frequently. Refresh manually or on schedule.

advanced.question: What is a full-text search index?

advanced.correctAnswer: B) Specialized index for natural language text search — much faster than LIKE '%term%'
advanced.explanation: PostgreSQL tsvector/tsquery for full-text. LIKE '%word%' can't use regular B-tree indexes.

advanced.question: What is database partitioning?
options: B) Dividing large table into smaller pieces on same server — by range, list, or hash
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Dividing large table into smaller pieces on same server — by range, list, or hash
advanced.explanation: Partition by date range for time-series data — queries only scan relevant partition.

beginner.question: What is Big O notation?
options: B) Describes worst-case time/space complexity growth rate as input size increases
beginner.correctAnswer: B) Describes worst-case time/space complexity growth rate as input size increases
beginner.explanation: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ) — memorize this order.

beginner.question: What is binary search and its requirements?
options: B) O(log n) search requiring sorted array — eliminate half search space each iteration
beginner.correctAnswer: B) O(log n) search requiring sorted array — eliminate half search space each iteration
beginner.explanation: Template: lo=0, hi=n-1, mid=(lo+hi)//2, check mid, move lo or hi. Many variations exist.

intermediate.question: What is the two-pointer technique?
options: B) Two indices moving through array solving problems in O(n) instead of O(n²)
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Two indices moving through array solving problems in O(n) instead of O(n²)
intermediate.explanation: Used for: palindrome check, container with most water, remove duplicates, sorted array sums.

intermediate.question: What is the sliding window technique?
options: B) Maintain subarray window sliding through input to avoid recomputation — O(n) not O(n²)
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Maintain subarray window sliding through input to avoid recomputation — O(n) not O(n²)
intermediate.explanation: Used for: max sum subarray, longest substring without repeat, minimum window substring.

intermediate.question: What is Floyd's cycle detection algorithm?
options: B) Slow (1 step) and fast (2 steps) pointers — they meet if cycle exists in linked list
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Slow (1 step) and fast (2 steps) pointers — they meet if cycle exists in linked list
intermediate.explanation: Also finds cycle start: when they meet, move one pointer to head, advance both 1 step to find entry.

intermediate.question: What is BST property?
options: B) Left subtree < root < right subtree — enables O(log n) search in balanced BST
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Left subtree < root < right subtree — enables O(log n) search in balanced BST
intermediate.explanation: BST inorder traversal produces sorted sequence. Unbalanced BST degrades to O(n).

intermediate.question: What is the difference between BFS and DFS?
options: B) BFS uses queue explores level-by-level; DFS uses stack/recursion explores depth-first
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) BFS uses queue explores level-by-level; DFS uses stack/recursion explores depth-first
intermediate.explanation: BFS for shortest path in unweighted graph. DFS for cycle detection, topological sort, connected components.

intermediate.question: What is amortized O(1) for dynamic array append?
options: B) Most appends O(1); occasional resize is O(n) — averages to O(1) over many operations
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Most appends O(1); occasional resize is O(n) — averages to O(1) over many operations
intermediate.explanation: Dynamic arrays double size on resize — doubling strategy gives amortized O(1) per append.

intermediate.question: What is prefix sum technique?
options: B) Precomputing cumulative sums for O(1) range sum queries after O(n) preprocessing
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Precomputing cumulative sums for O(1) range sum queries after O(n) preprocessing
intermediate.explanation: prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + arr[i]. Range sum [l,r] = prefix[r] - prefix[l-1] in O(1).

intermediate.question: What is a heap and its operations complexity?
options: B) Complete binary tree: insert O(log n), extract-min/max O(log n), peek O(1)
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Complete binary tree: insert O(log n), extract-min/max O(log n), peek O(1)
intermediate.explanation: Heaps are best for priority queues. Python heapq and Java PriorityQueue use min-heap.

intermediate.question: What is merge sort time/space complexity?
options: B) O(n log n) time, O(n) space — stable sort, consistent performance
C) O(n log n) time, O(log n) space
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) O(n log n) time, O(n) space — stable sort, consistent performance
intermediate.explanation: Merge sort always O(n log n) — unlike quicksort which degrades to O(n²) with bad pivots.

intermediate.question: What is quicksort average vs worst case?
options: B) Average O(n log n); worst O(n²) with sorted input and bad pivot selection
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Average O(n log n); worst O(n²) with sorted input and bad pivot selection
intermediate.explanation: Randomize pivot or use median-of-3 to avoid worst case. In-place O(log n) stack space.

advanced.question: What is a monotonic stack used for?
options: B) Stack maintaining monotonic order — used for next greater/smaller element in O(n)
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Stack maintaining monotonic order — used for next greater/smaller element in O(n)
advanced.explanation: Pattern: pop when current element violates monotonic property. Used in: daily temperatures, largest rectangle.

advanced.question: What is dynamic programming?
options: B) Optimization breaking problem into overlapping subproblems — memoization or tabulation
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Optimization breaking problem into overlapping subproblems — memoization or tabulation
advanced.explanation: DP identifies: optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems. Top-down=memoization, bottom-up=tabulation.

advanced.question: What is Dijkstra's algorithm?
options: B) Shortest path from source to all vertices in weighted non-negative graph using priority queue
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Shortest path from source to all vertices in weighted non-negative graph using priority queue
advanced.explanation: Time: O((V+E)log V) with binary heap. Fails with negative weights — use Bellman-Ford instead.

advanced.question: What is a trie (prefix tree)?
options: B) Tree storing strings character-by-character — O(L) insert/search where L is string length
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Tree storing strings character-by-character — O(L) insert/search where L is string length
advanced.explanation: Tries excel at: autocomplete, spell check, word search in grid, prefix matching.

advanced.question: What is topological sort?
options: B) Linear ordering of DAG vertices where u→v means u appears before v
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Linear ordering of DAG vertices where u→v means u appears before v
advanced.explanation: Used for: build systems, course scheduling, dependency resolution. Kahn's (BFS) or DFS-based.

advanced.question: What is Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)?
options: B) Tracks elements in disjoint sets with union and find operations in near O(1) amortized
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Tracks elements in disjoint sets with union and find operations in near O(1) amortized
advanced.explanation: Path compression + union by rank gives inverse Ackermann O(α(n)) — practically O(1). Used for Kruskal's MST.

advanced.question: What is a segment tree?
options: B) Binary tree for range queries (min/max/sum) and point updates in O(log n)
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Binary tree for range queries (min/max/sum) and point updates in O(log n)
advanced.explanation: Segment trees beat prefix sum when updates are frequent — both query and update are O(log n).

advanced.question: What is backtracking?
options: B) Explore all possibilities incrementally, abandon (backtrack) when constraint violated
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Explore all possibilities incrementally, abandon (backtrack) when constraint violated

beginner.question: What is TypeScript?

beginner.question: What is a union type?

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Prevents property reassignment after initialization — shallow immutability

intermediate.question: What is strict mode in tsconfig?

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Makes object/array deeply readonly with literal types — prevents widening to string/number
intermediate.explanation: const DIRECTIONS = ['north','south'] as const — type is readonly ['north','south'] not string[]

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) TS errors when object literal has extra properties not in target type — only on direct assignment

advanced.question: What is a type guard?

advanced.correctAnswer: B) Validates expression matches type while preserving narrowest type — TypeScript 4.9+

advanced.question: What is declaration merging?

beginner.question: What is the difference between list and tuple?

beginner.explanation: Use tuples for data that shouldn't change (coordinates, RGB). Lists for collections that need modification.

beginner.correctAnswer: B) is checks identity (same object in memory); == checks equality (same value)

beginner.question: What is f-string vs .format() vs %?

intermediate.question: What is a Python decorator?

intermediate.question: What is the difference between deep and shallow copy?

advanced.question: What is the GIL in Python?

beginner.question: What is the OSI model?

beginner.question: What is the difference between TCP and UDP?

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Maps private IP addresses to public IP — allows many devices to share one public IP

advanced.question: What is HTTP/2 vs HTTP/1.1?

beginner.question: What is SQL injection?

beginner.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?

intermediate.question: What is XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)?

intermediate.question: What is a man-in-the-middle attack?

intermediate.question: What is OWASP Top 10?

intermediate.question: What is a hash function in cryptography?

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) One-way function mapping arbitrary input to fixed-size digest — deterministic, collision resistant

intermediate.question: What is IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference)?

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Multiple layered security controls — no single point of failure protects entire system

intermediate.question: What is a rainbow table attack?

advanced.question: What is privilege escalation?

advanced.question: What is a JWT vulnerability when using none algorithm?

advanced.question: What is threat modeling?

beginner.question: What is supervised vs unsupervised learning?

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Optimization algorithm iteratively adjusting parameters in direction of steepest loss decrease

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Algorithm computing gradients layer-by-layer using chain rule — enables training deep networks

intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Using pre-trained model on new task — fine-tune existing weights rather than train from scratch

intermediate.question: What is regularization?
options: B) Techniques reducing overfitting: L1/L2 add penalty to loss; dropout randomly zeros neurons
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Techniques reducing overfitting: L1/L2 add penalty to loss; dropout randomly zeros neurons
intermediate.explanation: L1 (Lasso): sparsity, feature selection. L2 (Ridge): shrink weights. Dropout: ensemble effect.

intermediate.question: What is RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)?
options: B) Enhancing LLM with retrieved relevant documents — grounds responses in factual external knowledge
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Enhancing LLM with retrieved relevant documents — grounds responses in factual external knowledge
intermediate.explanation: Fetch relevant docs via vector search → inject into context → LLM generates grounded answer. Reduces hallucination.

intermediate.question: What is fine-tuning vs prompting?
options: B) Fine-tuning: update model weights on task-specific data; Prompting: guide behavior via input only
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Fine-tuning: update model weights on task-specific data; Prompting: guide behavior via input only
intermediate.explanation: Try prompting first — cheaper and faster. Fine-tune when prompting can't achieve needed quality/style.

intermediate.question: What is a vector embedding?
options: B) Dense numerical representation of data in high-dimensional space — semantic similarity = vector proximity
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Dense numerical representation of data in high-dimensional space — semantic similarity = vector proximity
intermediate.explanation: word2vec: king - man + woman ≈ queen. Text embeddings power semantic search and RAG.

intermediate.question: What is a vector database?
options: B) Specialized database for similarity search on embeddings — Pinecone, Chroma, Weaviate, pgvector
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Specialized database for similarity search on embeddings — Pinecone, Chroma, Weaviate, pgvector
intermediate.explanation: Stores vectors and finds nearest neighbors efficiently using HNSW or IVF indexes.

intermediate.question: What is temperature in LLMs?
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Controls randomness of output — 0=deterministic, 1=balanced, >1=creative/random
intermediate.explanation: Low temperature for factual tasks (0.1-0.3). High for creative writing (0.7-1.0). Applied to softmax logits.

intermediate.question: What is the context window in LLMs?
options: B) Maximum tokens model can process at once — determines how much text model 'remembers'
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Maximum tokens model can process at once — determines how much text model 'remembers'
intermediate.explanation: GPT-4: 128k tokens. Claude: 200k. Longer context = more expensive. RAG extends effective context.

intermediate.question: What is chain-of-thought prompting?
options: B) Prompting LLM to show reasoning steps before answer — dramatically improves complex problem solving
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Prompting LLM to show reasoning steps before answer — dramatically improves complex problem solving
intermediate.explanation: 'Let's think step by step...' or few-shot examples with reasoning. Enables models to solve math/logic.

advanced.question: What is the transformer architecture?
options: B) Attention-based architecture using self-attention mechanisms — foundation of all modern LLMs
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Attention-based architecture using self-attention mechanisms — foundation of all modern LLMs
advanced.explanation: Self-attention allows attending to all positions simultaneously. Multi-head attention captures different relationships.

advanced.question: What is attention mechanism in transformers?
options: B) Query-Key-Value mechanism: attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k)V — each token attends to all others
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Query-Key-Value mechanism: attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k)V — each token attends to all others
advanced.explanation: √d_k scaling prevents dot products from getting too large in high dimensions.

advanced.question: What is batch normalization?
options: B) Normalizes layer inputs per mini-batch — reduces internal covariate shift, speeds training
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Normalizes layer inputs per mini-batch — reduces internal covariate shift, speeds training
advanced.explanation: Apply after linear layer, before activation. Allows higher learning rates, acts as regularizer.

advanced.question: What is the vanishing gradient problem?
options: B) Gradients become exponentially small in early layers — training stalls in deep networks
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Gradients become exponentially small in early layers — training stalls in deep networks
advanced.explanation: Solutions: ReLU activations, residual connections (ResNet), batch normalization, LSTM for sequences.

advanced.question: What is prompt injection?
options: B) Attacker manipulates LLM behavior through carefully crafted inputs overriding system instructions
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Attacker manipulates LLM behavior through carefully crafted inputs overriding system instructions
advanced.explanation: 'Ignore previous instructions...' — LLMs can't cryptographically separate instructions from data yet.

advanced.question: What is RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)?
options: B) Training LLMs to align with human preferences using human evaluators and PPO algorithm
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Training LLMs to align with human preferences using human evaluators and PPO algorithm
advanced.explanation: Used by OpenAI for ChatGPT. Humans rank responses → reward model trained → LLM optimized via PPO.

advanced.question: What is LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)?
options: B) Parameter-efficient fine-tuning — adds small trainable matrices, keeps base model frozen
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Parameter-efficient fine-tuning — adds small trainable matrices, keeps base model frozen
advanced.explanation: LoRA reduces trainable params by 10,000x — fine-tune GPT on consumer GPU. QLoRA adds 4-bit quantization.

beginner.question: What is an AI agent?
options: B) Autonomous system perceiving environment, making decisions, taking actions to achieve goals
beginner.correctAnswer: B) Autonomous system perceiving environment, making decisions, taking actions to achieve goals
beginner.explanation: Agents combine LLMs with tools, memory, and planning to autonomously complete multi-step tasks.

beginner.question: What is a tool in agent context?
options: B) Function agent can call to interact with world — web search, code execution, DB queries, APIs
beginner.correctAnswer: B) Function agent can call to interact with world — web search, code execution, DB queries, APIs
beginner.explanation: Tools are agent's hands. Careful tool design with clear descriptions improves LLM tool selection accuracy.

beginner.question: What is an agent loop / agentic loop?
options: B) Observe → Think → Act cycle repeating until goal achieved or stop condition met
beginner.correctAnswer: B) Observe → Think → Act cycle repeating until goal achieved or stop condition met
beginner.explanation: Each iteration: get observations, reason about state, pick action, execute, observe result, repeat.

intermediate.question: What is ReAct (Reasoning + Acting)?
options: B) Agent pattern interleaving reasoning traces and actions — LLM thinks then acts iteratively
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Agent pattern interleaving reasoning traces and actions — LLM thinks then acts iteratively
intermediate.explanation: Thought: I need to search X. Action: search(X). Observation: result. Thought: now I know... — iterative loop.

intermediate.question: What is tool calling / function calling in agents?
options: B) LLM outputs structured JSON describing function to call — framework executes and returns result
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) LLM outputs structured JSON describing function to call — framework executes and returns result
intermediate.explanation: LLM decides: {name: 'search', args: {query: 'weather NYC'}}. Framework calls search(), returns to LLM.

intermediate.question: What is agent memory architecture?
options: B) Short-term (context window), long-term (vector DB), episodic (past interactions), semantic (facts)
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Short-term (context window), long-term (vector DB), episodic (past interactions), semantic (facts)
intermediate.explanation: Short-term limited by context. Long-term via RAG retrieval. Episodic stores past conversations.

intermediate.question: What is LangChain?
options: B) Framework for building LLM applications — chains, agents, tools, memory, RAG abstractions
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Framework for building LLM applications — chains, agents, tools, memory, RAG abstractions
intermediate.explanation: LangChain provides standardized interfaces for LLMs, vector stores, tools enabling complex pipelines.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between chain and agent in LangChain?
options: B) Chain: fixed predetermined steps; Agent: LLM dynamically decides which tools to use
D) Chain uses LLM; Agent doesn't
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Chain: fixed predetermined steps; Agent: LLM dynamically decides which tools to use
intermediate.explanation: Use chains for predictable workflows. Use agents when decision logic is complex or dynamic.

intermediate.question: What is structured output in agents?
options: B) Constraining LLM output to specific schema — ensures parseable reliable tool calls and decisions
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Constraining LLM output to specific schema — ensures parseable reliable tool calls and decisions
intermediate.explanation: Pydantic models, JSON schema, instructor library — force LLM to output valid structured data.

intermediate.question: What is a system prompt in agent design?
options: B) Persistent instructions defining agent identity, capabilities, constraints, and behavioral guidelines
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Persistent instructions defining agent identity, capabilities, constraints, and behavioral guidelines
intermediate.explanation: Well-crafted system prompts are crucial for agent reliability. Define role, tools, output format, safety rules.

intermediate.question: What is OpenAI Assistants API?
options: B) Managed agent infrastructure — threads, messages, runs, tool execution handled by OpenAI
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Managed agent infrastructure — threads, messages, runs, tool execution handled by OpenAI
intermediate.explanation: Handles: persistent threads, file attachments, code interpreter, retrieval, function calling — batteries included.

intermediate.question: What is task decomposition in agents?
options: B) Breaking complex task into subtasks assignable to tools or sub-agents — enables complex automation
intermediate.correctAnswer: B) Breaking complex task into subtasks assignable to tools or sub-agents — enables complex automation
intermediate.explanation: 'Write a research report' → search, read papers, synthesize, write sections, review — decomposed steps.

advanced.question: What is the ReAct vs Plan-and-Execute pattern?
options: B) ReAct: interleaved think-act; Plan-and-Execute: plan entire sequence first, then execute steps
advanced.correctAnswer: B) ReAct: interleaved think-act; Plan-and-Execute: plan entire sequence first, then execute steps
advanced.explanation: Plan-and-Execute better for long tasks with clear steps. ReAct better for exploratory/adaptive tasks.

advanced.question: What is a multi-agent system?
options: B) Multiple specialized agents collaborating — each has specific role, communicate to solve complex tasks
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Multiple specialized agents collaborating — each has specific role, communicate to solve complex tasks
advanced.explanation: AutoGen, CrewAI patterns: orchestrator delegates to specialist agents (researcher, coder, reviewer).

advanced.question: What is agent hallucination and mitigation?
options: B) Agent confidently takes wrong action — mitigate with tool verification, human-in-loop, self-reflection
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Agent confidently takes wrong action — mitigate with tool verification, human-in-loop, self-reflection
advanced.explanation: Grounding via tools, retrieval, structured outputs reduce hallucination. Human approval for irreversible actions.

advanced.question: What is agent evaluation (evals)?
options: B) Systematic measurement of agent performance — task completion, efficiency, accuracy, safety
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Systematic measurement of agent performance — task completion, efficiency, accuracy, safety
advanced.explanation: Build eval harness before optimizing. Track: success rate, tool call efficiency, hallucination rate, cost.

advanced.question: What is the AutoGen framework?
options: B) Microsoft framework for multi-agent conversations — agents converse with each other to solve tasks
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Microsoft framework for multi-agent conversations — agents converse with each other to solve tasks
advanced.explanation: AssistantAgent + UserProxyAgent pattern. Agents can write and execute code collaboratively.

advanced.question: What is the Model Context Protocol (MCP)?
options: B) Anthropic's standardized protocol for connecting LLMs to tools and data sources
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Anthropic's standardized protocol for connecting LLMs to tools and data sources
advanced.explanation: MCP standardizes how AI assistants connect to tools — like USB-C for AI. Enables reusable tool servers.

advanced.question: What is semantic kernel?
options: B) Microsoft SDK connecting LLMs with plugins and memories for AI application development
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Microsoft SDK connecting LLMs with plugins and memories for AI application development
advanced.explanation: SK provides skills/plugins, memory connectors, planners for enterprise AI applications in C#, Python, Java.

advanced.question: What is self-reflection in AI agents?
options: B) Agent critiques its own outputs and plans, iterating to improve before final answer
advanced.correctAnswer: B) Agent critiques its own outputs and plans, iterating to improve before final answer
advanced.explanation: Reflexion paper: agent reflects on failures, stores reflections in memory, avoids repeating mistakes.

HTML.question: What does HTML stand for?
HTML.correctAnswer: A) Hyper Text Markup Language
HTML.explanation: HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language — the standard language for creating web pages.

HTML.question: Which tag creates a hyperlink?
HTML.correctAnswer: B) <a>
HTML.explanation: The <a> (anchor) tag with href attribute creates hyperlinks.

HTML.question: What is the correct HTML for the largest heading?
HTML.explanation: <h1> is the largest heading tag, <h6> is the smallest.

HTML.question: Which HTML attribute specifies an alternate text for an image?
HTML.correctAnswer: C) alt
HTML.explanation: The alt attribute provides alternative text for screen readers and when image fails to load.

HTML.question: What does the DOCTYPE declaration do?
options: B) Tells browser which HTML version is being used
HTML.correctAnswer: B) Tells browser which HTML version is being used
HTML.explanation: <!DOCTYPE html> tells the browser to use HTML5 rendering mode.

HTML.question: Which tag is used for an unordered list?
HTML.correctAnswer: C) <ul>
HTML.explanation: <ul> creates unordered (bulleted) lists, <ol> creates ordered (numbered) lists.

HTML.question: What is semantic HTML?
options: B) Using HTML elements that convey meaning about content structure
HTML.correctAnswer: B) Using HTML elements that convey meaning about content structure
HTML.explanation: Semantic elements like <article>, <nav>, <section> describe their content, improving accessibility and SEO.

HTML.question: Which attribute makes an input field required?
HTML.correctAnswer: C) required
HTML.explanation: The required attribute prevents form submission if the field is empty.

HTML.question: What is the difference between <div> and <span>?
options: B) div is block-level; span is inline
C) span is block-level; div is inline
D) div is for text; span is for images
HTML.correctAnswer: B) div is block-level; span is inline
HTML.explanation: Block elements start on new line and take full width. Inline elements flow within text.

HTML.question: What does the <meta charset='UTF-8'> tag do?
options: B) Defines character encoding for the document
HTML.correctAnswer: B) Defines character encoding for the document
HTML.explanation: UTF-8 encoding supports virtually all characters and is the standard for web pages.

HTML.question: Which HTML5 element defines navigation links?
HTML.correctAnswer: C) <nav>
HTML.explanation: <nav> is a semantic element specifically for navigation menus and links.

HTML.question: What is the purpose of the <figure> element?
options: B) Self-contained content like images/diagrams with optional caption
HTML.correctAnswer: B) Self-contained content like images/diagrams with optional caption
HTML.explanation: <figure> with <figcaption> provides semantic grouping for media content with descriptions.

HTML.question: What is ARIA in HTML?
options: B) Accessible Rich Internet Applications — attributes improving accessibility
HTML.correctAnswer: B) Accessible Rich Internet Applications — attributes improving accessibility
HTML.explanation: ARIA attributes like role, aria-label, aria-hidden help screen readers understand page structure.

HTML.question: What is the difference between id and class attributes?
options: B) id must be unique per page; class can be reused on multiple elements
C) class must be unique; id can repeat
D) id is for CSS; class is for JavaScript
HTML.correctAnswer: B) id must be unique per page; class can be reused on multiple elements
HTML.explanation: Multiple elements can share a class but each id must be unique within a document.

HTML.question: What does the async attribute do on a <script> tag?
options: A) Runs script after page loads
B) Script downloads asynchronously and executes immediately when ready — doesn't block parsing
C) Makes script run in worker thread
D) Defers script to end of body
HTML.correctAnswer: B) Script downloads asynchronously and executes immediately when ready — doesn't block parsing
HTML.explanation: async downloads script without blocking, executes when ready. defer waits for HTML parsing to complete.

HTML.question: What is the difference between defer and async script loading?
options: B) async executes as soon as downloaded; defer waits for DOM parsing to complete before executing
HTML.correctAnswer: B) async executes as soon as downloaded; defer waits for DOM parsing to complete before executing
HTML.explanation: Use defer for scripts that depend on DOM. Use async for independent scripts like analytics.

HTML.question: What is Content Security Policy (CSP)?
options: B) HTTP header controlling what resources browser can load — prevents XSS attacks
HTML.correctAnswer: B) HTTP header controlling what resources browser can load — prevents XSS attacks
HTML.explanation: CSP via meta tag or HTTP header restricts sources of scripts, styles, images to prevent injection attacks.

HTML.question: What does the <template> element do?
options: A) Creates HTML templates for emails
B) Holds client-side HTML not rendered until activated via JavaScript
HTML.correctAnswer: B) Holds client-side HTML not rendered until activated via JavaScript
HTML.explanation: <template> content is inert — not rendered, no scripts run — until cloned into DOM via JS.

HTML.question: What is the Shadow DOM?
options: B) Encapsulated DOM subtree for Web Components — styles and scripts don't leak in/out
HTML.correctAnswer: B) Encapsulated DOM subtree for Web Components — styles and scripts don't leak in/out
HTML.explanation: Shadow DOM provides encapsulation for custom elements, preventing style conflicts.

HTML.question: What is a Web Component?
options: B) Suite of browser APIs to create custom, reusable HTML elements — Custom Elements + Shadow DOM + Templates
HTML.correctAnswer: B) Suite of browser APIs to create custom, reusable HTML elements — Custom Elements + Shadow DOM + Templates
HTML.explanation: Web Components are framework-agnostic reusable elements using native browser APIs.

CSS.question: What is the CSS box model?
options: B) content + padding + border + margin — defines element's space and dimensions
CSS.correctAnswer: B) content + padding + border + margin — defines element's space and dimensions
CSS.explanation: Every element is a box. Understanding content/padding/border/margin is fundamental to layout.

CSS.question: What is the difference between box-sizing: content-box vs border-box?
options: B) content-box: width=content only; border-box: width includes padding+border
D) content-box is default in modern browsers
CSS.correctAnswer: B) content-box: width=content only; border-box: width includes padding+border
CSS.explanation: border-box makes sizing intuitive — a 200px box is always 200px regardless of padding/border.

CSS.question: What is CSS specificity order (highest to lowest)?
CSS.correctAnswer: B) inline > ID > class/pseudo-class > element
CSS.explanation: Inline styles override all. ID selectors > class selectors > element selectors.

CSS.question: What is the difference between Flexbox and Grid?
options: B) Flexbox is 1D (row or column); Grid is 2D (rows AND columns simultaneously)
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Flexbox is 1D (row or column); Grid is 2D (rows AND columns simultaneously)
CSS.explanation: Use Flexbox for single-axis layouts. Use Grid for complex 2D page layouts.

CSS.question: What does position: sticky do?
options: B) Scrolls normally until hitting threshold, then sticks in place
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Scrolls normally until hitting threshold, then sticks in place
CSS.explanation: Sticky is a hybrid of relative and fixed — sticks within its scroll container.

CSS.question: What is a CSS pseudo-element?
options: B) Keywords like ::before, ::after that style specific parts of element
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Keywords like ::before, ::after that style specific parts of element
CSS.explanation: ::before and ::after inject content before/after element. ::first-line styles first line of text.

CSS.question: What is z-index and what does it require?
options: B) Controls stacking order — only works on positioned elements (not static)
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Controls stacking order — only works on positioned elements (not static)
CSS.explanation: For z-index to work, element must have position: relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky.

CSS.question: What is a CSS custom property (variable)?
options: B) --name: value variables accessed with var(--name), scoped to selector
CSS.correctAnswer: B) --name: value variables accessed with var(--name), scoped to selector
CSS.explanation: CSS variables enable theming, are reactive to changes, and cascade through the DOM.

CSS.question: What is the difference between em and rem units?
options: B) em relative to parent's font-size; rem relative to root (html) font-size
C) rem is relative to parent
CSS.correctAnswer: B) em relative to parent's font-size; rem relative to root (html) font-size
CSS.explanation: rem is more predictable for font-sizes as it doesn't compound with nested elements.

CSS.question: What is CSS transform vs transition?
options: B) transform changes geometry (rotate/scale/translate); transition animates property change over time
CSS.correctAnswer: B) transform changes geometry (rotate/scale/translate); transition animates property change over time
CSS.explanation: Combine both: use transform for what to change, transition for how long the change takes.

CSS.question: What is CSS Grid fr unit?
options: B) Fractional unit — represents fraction of available space in grid container
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Fractional unit — represents fraction of available space in grid container
CSS.explanation: 1fr 2fr gives columns ratio 1:2 of available space after fixed columns are allocated.

CSS.question: What does will-change property do?
options: B) Hints browser about upcoming changes — allows GPU layer optimization in advance
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Hints browser about upcoming changes — allows GPU layer optimization in advance
CSS.explanation: Use will-change: transform on animated elements to trigger GPU compositing layer.

CSS.question: What is BEM naming methodology?
options: B) Block__Element--Modifier — naming convention preventing style conflicts
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Block__Element--Modifier — naming convention preventing style conflicts
CSS.explanation: BEM creates predictable, maintainable CSS by defining clear naming relationships.

CSS.question: What is CSS containment (contain property)?
options: B) Tells browser element is independent — enables performance optimizations by limiting reflow scope
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Tells browser element is independent — enables performance optimizations by limiting reflow scope
CSS.explanation: contain: layout|style|paint|size tells browser changes inside don't affect outside.

CSS.question: What is the difference between visibility:hidden and display:none?
options: B) visibility:hidden hides but keeps space; display:none removes element and collapses space
CSS.correctAnswer: B) visibility:hidden hides but keeps space; display:none removes element and collapses space
CSS.explanation: visibility:hidden is like making element transparent. display:none removes it from layout completely.

CSS.question: What is CSS Grid auto-fit vs auto-fill?
options: B) auto-fill keeps empty tracks; auto-fit collapses empty tracks — both with minmax() create responsive grids
C) auto-fit is for images only
CSS.correctAnswer: B) auto-fill keeps empty tracks; auto-fit collapses empty tracks — both with minmax() create responsive grids
CSS.explanation: grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(200px, 1fr)) creates responsive layouts without media queries.

CSS.question: What is the cascade in CSS?
options: B) Algorithm determining which CSS rule applies when multiple rules target same element
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Algorithm determining which CSS rule applies when multiple rules target same element
CSS.explanation: Cascade considers specificity, source order, importance, and inheritance to resolve conflicts.

CSS.question: What is CSS logical properties?
options: B) Writing-mode independent properties: margin-inline instead of margin-left/right
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Writing-mode independent properties: margin-inline instead of margin-left/right
CSS.explanation: Logical properties adapt to writing direction automatically, better for international/RTL layouts.

CSS.question: What is the @layer rule?
options: B) Defines cascade layers to control specificity without specificity wars
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Defines cascade layers to control specificity without specificity wars
CSS.explanation: @layer lets you organize styles into layers where earlier layers have lower specificity.

CSS.question: What is CSS Houdini?
options: B) Low-level CSS APIs giving developers direct access to CSS rendering engine
CSS.correctAnswer: B) Low-level CSS APIs giving developers direct access to CSS rendering engine
CSS.explanation: Houdini APIs like Paint API, Layout API let developers extend CSS itself with JavaScript.

JavaScript.question: What is the output of typeof null?
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) object
JavaScript.explanation: This is a famous JS bug. typeof null returns 'object' — has been kept for backward compatibility.

JavaScript.question: What is a closure?
options: B) Function retaining access to outer scope variables even after outer function returns
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Function retaining access to outer scope variables even after outer function returns
JavaScript.explanation: Closures enable data encapsulation, module patterns, and callbacks with preserved context.

JavaScript.question: What is the difference between let, const, and var?
options: B) var is function-scoped hoisted; let/const are block-scoped; const can't be reassigned
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) var is function-scoped hoisted; let/const are block-scoped; const can't be reassigned
JavaScript.explanation: Prefer const by default, let when reassignment needed, avoid var due to hoisting confusion.

JavaScript.question: What is event delegation?
options: B) Adding single listener to parent to handle events from all children via bubbling
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Adding single listener to parent to handle events from all children via bubbling
JavaScript.explanation: Event delegation improves performance for dynamic lists — one handler instead of hundreds.

JavaScript.question: What is the prototype chain?
options: B) Mechanism where objects inherit properties from their prototype object up the chain to null
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Mechanism where objects inherit properties from their prototype object up the chain to null
JavaScript.explanation: When accessing property, JS looks up prototype chain until found or reaches null.

JavaScript.question: What is the event loop?
options: B) Single-threaded mechanism: call stack + web APIs + callback queue + microtask queue
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Single-threaded mechanism: call stack + web APIs + callback queue + microtask queue
JavaScript.explanation: Event loop enables non-blocking I/O by offloading to Web APIs and processing callbacks.

JavaScript.question: Microtasks vs macrotasks — execution order?
options: B) After each task: ALL microtasks drain first (Promises), then one macrotask (setTimeout)
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) After each task: ALL microtasks drain first (Promises), then one macrotask (setTimeout)
JavaScript.explanation: Promise.then callbacks are microtasks — they run before setTimeout callbacks always.

JavaScript.question: What does async/await compile to?
options: B) Syntactic sugar over Promises — async returns Promise, await pauses execution until resolved
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Syntactic sugar over Promises — async returns Promise, await pauses execution until resolved
JavaScript.explanation: async/await makes async code readable. Under the hood, it still uses Promises.

JavaScript.question: What is the difference between == and ===?
options: B) == coerces types then compares; === compares value AND type without coercion
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) == coerces types then compares; === compares value AND type without coercion
JavaScript.explanation: Always use === to avoid subtle bugs from type coercion.

JavaScript.question: What is memoization?
options: B) Optimization caching function results — same inputs return cached output avoiding recomputation
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Optimization caching function results — same inputs return cached output avoiding recomputation
JavaScript.explanation: Useful for expensive pure functions called repeatedly with same arguments.

JavaScript.question: What is currying?
options: B) Transforming f(a,b,c) into f(a)(b)(c) — each call takes one arg and returns function
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Transforming f(a,b,c) into f(a)(b)(c) — each call takes one arg and returns function
JavaScript.explanation: Currying enables partial application — pre-fill some arguments and reuse the specialized function.

JavaScript.question: What is a pure function?
options: B) Always produces same output for same input AND has zero side effects
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Always produces same output for same input AND has zero side effects
JavaScript.explanation: Pure functions are predictable, testable, and composable — cornerstone of functional programming.

JavaScript.question: What is the Proxy object?
options: B) Wraps target object intercepting fundamental operations (get/set/delete) via handlers
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Wraps target object intercepting fundamental operations (get/set/delete) via handlers
JavaScript.explanation: Proxies power reactive frameworks (Vue 3), validation, and object virtualization.

JavaScript.question: What is WeakMap vs Map?
options: B) WeakMap keys must be objects, weakly held — garbage collected when no other reference exists
D) WeakMap can't iterate (same as Map)
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) WeakMap keys must be objects, weakly held — garbage collected when no other reference exists
JavaScript.explanation: WeakMap prevents memory leaks for object metadata. Can't be iterated or checked for size.

JavaScript.question: What is tail call optimization?
options: B) Reusing current stack frame when last action is recursive call — prevents stack overflow
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Reusing current stack frame when last action is recursive call — prevents stack overflow
JavaScript.explanation: TCO allows infinite recursion without stack overflow if recursive call is in tail position.

JavaScript.question: What is the temporal dead zone?
options: B) Period where let/const is declared but not initialized — accessing throws ReferenceError
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Period where let/const is declared but not initialized — accessing throws ReferenceError
JavaScript.explanation: Unlike var (initialized to undefined), let/const throw if accessed before declaration.

JavaScript.question: What does Object.freeze() do?
options: B) Prevents adding/removing/modifying own properties — shallow freeze only
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Prevents adding/removing/modifying own properties — shallow freeze only
JavaScript.explanation: Freeze is shallow — nested objects are still mutable. For deep immutability, recurse manually.

JavaScript.question: What is a generator function?
options: B) Function using yield to pause/resume — returns iterator producing values lazily
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Function using yield to pause/resume — returns iterator producing values lazily
JavaScript.explanation: Generators enable infinite sequences, custom iterators, and cooperative multitasking.

JavaScript.question: What is Symbol.iterator?
options: B) Well-known symbol defining custom iteration — makes object work with for...of and spread
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Well-known symbol defining custom iteration — makes object work with for...of and spread
JavaScript.explanation: Implement [Symbol.iterator]() to make any object iterable with for...of loops.

JavaScript.question: What is the difference between deep and shallow clone?
options: B) Shallow clone copies top-level properties with shared references; deep clone recursively copies all nested objects
C) Deep is slower but same result
D) Shallow creates new object always
JavaScript.correctAnswer: B) Shallow clone copies top-level properties with shared references; deep clone recursively copies all nested objects
JavaScript.explanation: Use structuredClone() for deep clone, spread operator {...obj} for shallow clone.

NodeJS.question: What is Node.js?
options: B) JavaScript runtime built on V8 enabling server-side execution with non-blocking I/O
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) JavaScript runtime built on V8 enabling server-side execution with non-blocking I/O
NodeJS.explanation: Node's event-driven, non-blocking model makes it efficient for I/O-heavy applications.

NodeJS.question: What is the difference between require() and import?
options: B) require() is CommonJS synchronous dynamic; import is ES modules static async tree-shakeable
D) require() is for JSON only
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) require() is CommonJS synchronous dynamic; import is ES modules static async tree-shakeable
NodeJS.explanation: Use .mjs or 'type':'module' in package.json for ES modules in Node.

NodeJS.question: What is the EventEmitter?
options: B) Core class for event-driven architecture — on() to subscribe, emit() to trigger events
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Core class for event-driven architecture — on() to subscribe, emit() to trigger events
NodeJS.explanation: Most Node.js core modules extend EventEmitter — streams, HTTP server, etc.

NodeJS.question: What is a Node.js stream?
options: B) Abstract interface for reading/writing data in chunks — memory efficient for large files
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Abstract interface for reading/writing data in chunks — memory efficient for large files
NodeJS.explanation: 4 types: Readable, Writable, Duplex, Transform. Pipe them for efficient data processing.

NodeJS.question: What is the cluster module?
options: B) Creates child processes sharing server port — utilizes multiple CPU cores
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Creates child processes sharing server port — utilizes multiple CPU cores
NodeJS.explanation: Since Node is single-threaded, cluster module spawns workers equal to CPU cores for load balancing.

NodeJS.question: What is libuv in Node.js?
options: B) C library providing async I/O, event loop, and thread pool to Node.js
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) C library providing async I/O, event loop, and thread pool to Node.js
NodeJS.explanation: libuv's thread pool handles file system ops and DNS that can't be done asynchronously by OS.

NodeJS.question: What is the difference between process.nextTick() and setImmediate()?
options: B) nextTick runs before I/O callbacks (same phase); setImmediate runs in check phase after I/O
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) nextTick runs before I/O callbacks (same phase); setImmediate runs in check phase after I/O
NodeJS.explanation: Use nextTick for critical callbacks. Use setImmediate to yield to I/O callbacks first.

NodeJS.question: What is middleware in Express?
options: B) Function(req, res, next) executing between request and route handler
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Function(req, res, next) executing between request and route handler
NodeJS.explanation: Middleware chain processes request: authentication, logging, parsing, validation before handler.

NodeJS.question: What is JWT and its structure?
options: B) JSON Web Token: header.payload.signature — stateless authentication token
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) JSON Web Token: header.payload.signature — stateless authentication token
NodeJS.explanation: JWT is signed (not encrypted by default). Never store sensitive data in payload — it's base64 decoded easily.

NodeJS.question: What is bcrypt and why use it for passwords?
options: B) Password hashing with salt — deliberately slow to prevent brute force attacks
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Password hashing with salt — deliberately slow to prevent brute force attacks
NodeJS.explanation: bcrypt's cost factor makes it exponentially slower per guess — 12 rounds is standard.

NodeJS.question: What is CORS and how to handle it in Node?
options: B) Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — browser security policy; handled with cors middleware
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — browser security policy; handled with cors middleware
NodeJS.explanation: CORS headers tell browser which origins can access your API. Configure origin whitelist carefully.

NodeJS.question: What is rate limiting?
options: B) Restricting requests per IP/user in time window — prevents abuse and DoS attacks
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Restricting requests per IP/user in time window — prevents abuse and DoS attacks
NodeJS.explanation: Use express-rate-limit to apply per-route or global limits, optionally backed by Redis.

NodeJS.question: What is graceful shutdown in Node.js?
options: B) Listening for SIGTERM to finish active requests before stopping — prevents data loss
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Listening for SIGTERM to finish active requests before stopping — prevents data loss
NodeJS.explanation: Handle SIGTERM/SIGINT signals. Stop accepting new connections. Wait for active requests to complete.

NodeJS.question: What is the purpose of helmet.js?
options: B) Express middleware setting HTTP security headers — X-Frame-Options, CSP, HSTS etc.
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Express middleware setting HTTP security headers — X-Frame-Options, CSP, HSTS etc.
NodeJS.explanation: Helmet sets ~11 security headers by default preventing clickjacking, MIME sniffing, XSS.

NodeJS.question: What is a Worker Thread in Node.js?
options: B) True parallel thread for CPU-intensive tasks — unlike cluster, shares memory
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) True parallel thread for CPU-intensive tasks — unlike cluster, shares memory
NodeJS.explanation: Use Worker Threads for CPU-bound work (image processing, crypto). Use cluster for HTTP scaling.

NodeJS.question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous in Node context?
options: B) Sync blocks event loop preventing other operations; async offloads via callbacks/promises
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Sync blocks event loop preventing other operations; async offloads via callbacks/promises
NodeJS.explanation: Never use sync file/network operations in production Node servers — they block all other requests.

NodeJS.question: What is PM2?
options: B) Production process manager — clustering, zero-downtime reload, monitoring, log management
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Production process manager — clustering, zero-downtime reload, monitoring, log management
NodeJS.explanation: PM2 keeps Node apps alive, automatically restarts on crash, manages multiple app instances.

NodeJS.question: What is __dirname vs process.cwd()?
options: B) __dirname is directory of current module file; process.cwd() is current working directory when node started
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) __dirname is directory of current module file; process.cwd() is current working directory when node started
NodeJS.explanation: Use __dirname for paths relative to module file. process.cwd() changes based on where node is run from.

NodeJS.question: What is connection pooling in databases?
options: B) Pre-created reusable connections — avoids expensive connection setup per request
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Pre-created reusable connections — avoids expensive connection setup per request
NodeJS.explanation: Mongoose maintains connection pool automatically. Configure poolSize based on expected concurrency.

NodeJS.question: What is the N+1 query problem?
options: B) Fetching N parent records then N separate queries for children — O(N+1) total queries
NodeJS.correctAnswer: B) Fetching N parent records then N separate queries for children — O(N+1) total queries
NodeJS.explanation: Fix with populate() in Mongoose or JOIN in SQL — reduces to 2 queries regardless of N.
